EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm drifted northeast from the Atlantic waters to central Marathon, producing strong downburst winds out of the northeast across the east end of Boot Key Harbor and areas around the Sombrero Country Club.  While originally reported as a landfalling waterspout and tornado, wind damage was consistent with that of strong straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage to trees was noted along the back nine of the Sombrero Country Club, with lose objects and lawn furniture tossed from under stilted homes into Boot Key Harbor.  No damage was observed to roofing materials, doors or windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low developed along a northward moving front and positioned across central Florida. Strong isentropic life and high moisture coincided along the front over Flagler county where heavy rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County emergency management relayed storm total rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches across Palm Coast, with the highest amount of 3.5 inches measured in west-central Palm Coast. Elsewhere across the county, storm total amounts ranged from 3 inches in Bunnell to 4.30 inches in Haw Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding and a few reports of wind damage across portions of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down across County Road 162.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding and a few reports of wind damage across portions of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of Thomas Drive and Huff Street near Panama City Beach.  Water was one to three feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding and a few reports of wind damage across portions of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 98 from Avenue A to Industrial Road in Port St Joe was closed by flood water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding and a few reports of wind damage across portions of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported across Bottoms Road from U.S. Highway 98 to Normans Way.  Water was up to one foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eastern U.S. trough axis edged eastward and increased southwesterly flow over the forecast area. Cool 500 mb temperatures ranged from -8 to -10 degrees Celcius. Scattered severe storms developed across the area in the afternoon and evening along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail covered the ground at Atlantic Boulevard and University Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eastern U.S. trough axis edged eastward and increased southwesterly flow over the forecast area. Cool 500 mb temperatures ranged from -8 to -10 degrees Celcius. Scattered severe storms developed across the area in the afternoon and evening along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two houses on the 5000 block of Hecksher Drive sustained damage from fallen trees due to thunderstorm wind gusts. One house had severe damage to its backside from thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short wave trough energy combined over a moist and unstable airmass across north and central Florida to yield scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported dime to penny size hail and gusty winds. The time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long wave trough was over the eastern U.S. with a swath of moisture confined over central Florida and points southward. Subsidence and dry air was noted over Southeast Georgia and portions of Northeast Florida. A shortwave trough move across the area over an environment that consisted of 1.5 PWAT and modified CAPES of 4200 J/kg and a lifted index of -8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the northern portion of the county north of U.S. Highway 90 into the Taylor vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long wave trough was over the eastern U.S. with a swath of moisture confined over central Florida and points southward. Subsidence and dry air was noted over Southeast Georgia and portions of Northeast Florida. A shortwave trough move across the area over an environment that consisted of 1.5 PWAT and modified CAPES of 4200 J/kg and a lifted index of -8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Jennings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level trough axis extended down the Appalachian spine and into the lower Mississippi River Valley which provided a west to southwest steering flow over the local area. Precipitable water amounts were near 2 inches with 500 mb temperatures ranging from -8 degrees C to -10 degrees C. Scattered severe storms developed during the afternoon and evening along the sea breeze fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and blocked a road near Interstate 95. The time of the event was based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down along William Burgess Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak west-southwest flow allowed both sea breezes to be active with a collision likely near the U.S. Highway 301 corridor. Mid level dry air was noted as well as moderately cool 500 mb temperatures of -8 degrees C.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home on Russell Drive was struck by lightning with minimal damage reported. Cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak west-southwest flow allowed both sea breezes to be active with a collision likely near the U.S. Highway 301 corridor. Mid level dry air was noted as well as moderately cool 500 mb temperatures of -8 degrees C.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Buffalo Bill Drive was struck by lightning with minimal damage reported. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass existed over the area with a broad scale upper level trough over the region. Convection developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted about a half mile offshore about a half mile south of Fourth Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass existed over the area with a broad scale upper level trough over the region. Convection developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch branches were breaking off of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass existed over the area with a broad scale upper level trough over the region. Convection developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received from 890 Florida Power and Light customers without power in Grand Haven due to downed power lines from thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be 55 to 60 mph in west Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eastern U.S. trough axis edged eastward and increased southwesterly flow over the forecast area. Cool 500 mb temperatures ranged from -8 to -10 degrees Celcius. Scattered severe storms developed across the area in the afternoon and evening along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail fell along Monument Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm associated with a long-lived cell exhibiting rotation on radar produced damage to several trees and small structures on the Forever Florida Eco Ranch near Kenansville in rural Osceola County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey revealed several large oak trees toppled or split on the Forever Florida Eco Ranch on Highway 441 in rural Osceola County near Kenansville. Very minor wind damage occurred to a barn and a roof vent on the visitor center building. Several fences were damaged and two portable out buildings were blown onto their sides. Straight line winds were estimated at 50-60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thick fog developed across a large part of east central Florida during the early morning hours and lasted until shortly after sunrise. The fog becomes especially dense near and west of the Interstate 4 corridor, resulting in a serious vehicle accident. The accident also produced significant structural damage to a bridge, resulting in the closure of multiple lanes of the Florida turnpike for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense early morning fog contributed to a vehicle accident in Montverde within Lake County about 622 EST, according to Florida Highway Patrol officials. A man in a pickup truck drove through a stop sign and collided with a tractor-trailer. The tractor-trailer caught on fire, jackknifed, and struck a bridge below the Florida Turnpike, causing substantial structural damage to the bridge. Both drivers were injured. In addition, a fire truck crashed on its way to the accident scene, resulting in two additional injuries. Damage to the bridge caused both northbound lanes of the Florida Turnpike to be closed. One of the two northbound lanes was reopened two days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong storms developed across the area under weak steering flow as an upper level trough carved out across the eastern seaboard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motel in downtown Ocala reported dime to penny size hail. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active southwest flow tracked storms inland from the Gulf of Mexico in the morning, then a passing short wave trough further de-stabilized the atmosphere in the afternoon to produce widespread strong to severe storms. Low level winds of 30-35 knots yielded strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Gainesville television station reported 10 large trees were blown down across a field. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough and in concert with an approaching cold front combined over a moist and unstable airmass to yield scattered severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county road department reported numerous trees were blown down along Chester River Road. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving afternoon storms brought heavy rainfall to portions of Flagler county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters across the county reported high daily rainfall amounts. A spotter in South Central Palm Coast measured 5.00 inches of rainfall and another in West Central Palm Coast measured 3.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure north of Florida produced a moderate easterly wind flow across south Florida, leading to the formation of dangerous rip currents along the southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Canadian businessman drowned at Midtown Beach on Palm Beach as a result of rip currents. The 58-year-old man was swimming about 150 yards north of the guarded beach at Midtown Beach. ||Lifeguards and Fire-Rescue personnel performed CPR on the victim, which was then taken to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead.||Time of drowning is approximate and based on newspaper reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air in the wake of the heavy rains and severe flooding the previous night contributed to a very unstable atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving south across Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted by the public from Turkey Point Power Plant moving northeast just east of Homestead. The funnel cloud moved northeast and was observed by Homestead Air Reserve Base observers north of their location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air in the wake of the heavy rains and severe flooding the previous night contributed to a very unstable atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving south across Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was injured when her flower stand blew over during a strong wind gust at the corner of Biscayne Boulevard and NE 163 Street in North Miami Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, a funnel cloud formed over extreme southwest Melbourne, west of Interstate 95. A heavy shower was evident just south of the funnel and the rain area eventually built north to the location of the funnel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen observed a funnel cloud develop over extreme southeast Melbourne, south of Highway 192 and west of Interstate 95. Photographs of the funnel were taken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A teenage boy drowned after being caught in a rip current along a portion of the Volusia County beach without life guards. Volusia County Beach Patrol reported the presence of very strong rip currents on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 16 year old boy from New Smyrna Beach was caught in a rip current and drowned. |The boy entered the surf over a half mile away from the nearest life guard station. Volusia County Beach patrol reported that the boys mother stated that he could not swim well. Also, an elderly man was rescued from a rip current on another portion of the Volusia County beach and was taken to a local hospital and listed in stable condition.|Numerous other rip current rescues occurred along the County beaches during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm drifted northeast from the Atlantic waters to central Marathon, producing strong downburst winds out of the northeast across the east end of Boot Key Harbor and areas around the Sombrero Country Club.  While originally reported as a landfalling waterspout and tornado, wind damage was consistent with that of strong straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts out of the north northeast spread out of the east end of Boot Key Harbor.  Three small vessels, including one trimaran, were overturned.  A vessel docked at Dockside Marina measured a wind gust in excess of 100 mph at mast level.  An outdoor canopy secured by heavy tube steel framing was destroyed at a restaurant.  A tent was also carried into power lines, causing a power outage to most of the Sombrero Country Club and Sombrero Beach area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between high pressure off the southeast United States coast and Hurricane Ida over the northwestern Caribbean Sea and the southeast Gulf of Mexico produced strong winds over south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds of 25-30 mph persisted for over 2 days, with frequent gusts of 35-40 mph. Pompano Beach recorded a peak wind gust of 47 mph at 8 AM on November 9th. Other peak gusts include Virginia Key at 45 mph, Opa-Locka Airport at 40 mph and Palm Beach International Airport and Miami International Airport at 39 mph.||These winds produced power outages to approximately 3,000 customers across Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties. Some trees were reported to have toppled and/or uprooted, with the only confirmed report being a snapped pine tree which fell onto a car in Dania Beach, producing damage. Moderate beach erosion was also reported along some southeast Florida beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An already cold airmass was reinforced by even colder air behind a strong cold front. Low wind chills, freezing temperatures and frost affect most of east central Florida for three consecutive nights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures occurred for three consecutive nights. Wind chill readings in the 20s also occurred during the morning of the 21st. The coldest morning at most locations was on the 22nd, when hard freeze conditions spread south across many locations of the interior peninsula to Lake Okeechobee. Official temperature sites reached the mid to upper 20s, with a NWS Cooperative sites at Scottsmoor in NW Brevard county reaching 22 and Plymouth in NW Orange county reaching 16. Sub-freezing minimum readings and widespread frost occurred on the 23rd. Only the barrier islands south of Cape Canaveral remained at or slightly above freezing throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line traveling along a cold front moved through the area late in the evening of January 19th into the early morning hours of January 20th. This fast moving line produced wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph and one tornadic storm that also produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Lake Panasoffkee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass existed over the area with a broad scale upper level trough over the region. Convection developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Coast CERT members estimated wind gusts to 60 mph in West-Central Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale upper level trough continued over the eastern half of the country with a moist and unstable airmass over the local region. Mid level winds were around 30 to 40 knots with lifted indices near -8 deg C. Storms developed along the sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck homes in Palm Coast and Flagler Beach. Brush fires were also reported. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist airmass under prevailing southwest flow steered convection inland from the Gulf of Mexico throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted and persisted for about 1 minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms on the 31st brought flooding, wind damage and spawned a tornado across portions of the Florida Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were downed across Interstate 10 near mile marker 245.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms on the 31st brought flooding, wind damage and spawned a tornado across portions of the Florida Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large pine trees were down in a narrow convergent path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms on the 31st brought flooding, wind damage and spawned a tornado across portions of the Florida Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down on Crawfordville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm impacted Baker County on 3/1/2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small branches were blown down and a metal bracket holding rain gage was damaged due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended dry spell led to drought conditions and several wildfires across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida State Watch Office reported that the County Road 1474 wildfire near Hawthorne was about 300 acres and was 40 percent contained as of this morning. The fire started yesterday 3/18/09 at 546 p.m. local time in Beckhamtown, Florida. Three outbuildings were damaged by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms on the 31st brought flooding, wind damage and spawned a tornado across portions of the Florida Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down across Miccosukee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms on the 31st brought flooding, wind damage and spawned a tornado across portions of the Florida Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over State Highway 221 at Interstate 10 near the Texaco station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry April across South Florida led to the intensification of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: April continued the stretch of very dry conditions across South Florida. The five-month period from November 2008 to April 2009 was the second driest on record for most South Florida locations. April rainfall was less than an inch at most locations, with only metro sections of Southeast Florida receiving between 1.5 and 2.5 inches for the month.||Drought conditions spread and intensified across the region. Extreme drought (D3) were noted over interior South Florida, with severe drought (D2) noted over the remainder of mainland South Florida. The level of Lake Okeechobee fell from 12.2 feet at the beginning of the month to 11.1 feet by the end of the month, which was 2.5 feet below normal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southward into the central Florida Peninsula on Monday, May 18th with unseasonably strong high pressure building in behind it. An unseasonably strong non-tropical low pressure system developed over the Bahamas and began to track in a northwesterly direction toward the region. The strong pressure gradient between the high and low pressure systems brought strong winds to the coastal areas and excessive rainfall to portions of north Florida through Sunday, May 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant beach erosion was reported at Fort Clinch. There was barely space for a vehicle on a normally wide beach. Breakers of six to seven feet were estimated at that time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southward into the central Florida Peninsula on Monday, May 18th with unseasonably strong high pressure building in behind it. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall began in Flagler County on Sunday, May 17th associated with a cold frontal passage with between one and three inches of rain falling by Monday morning.  The rainfall increased on Monday with four and a half inches having fallen by 3 p.m. EDT/2 p.m. EST. Due to moist antecedent ground conditions flooding began quickly with County Road 35 west of Bunnell washed out near State Road 100. By Tuesday morning, the 19th, up to eleven and a half inches of rain had fallen with continuing flooding problems continuing. Heavy rainfall continued on Wednesday with up to 24 inches of rain having fallen since Sunday at the Fair Grounds. The rain finally began to slowly taper off on the 21st and 22nd; however, by the end of the event up to 28 inches of rainfall was reported by the school staff in downtown Bunnell (the County Seat). Extensive flooding was reported to multiple roads in Daytona North with over three feet of water on some. Several residents had to be rescued from flooded homes in that area. Significant roads that were reported as having been flooded and closed are County Road 75, County Road 65, Forest Park  Rd, Tangerine Rd,  County Road 15,  and Water Oak Road. In western Flagler County including Deanville, Andulusia, Cody���s Corner ���massive flooding��� of agricultural property was reported with many fields resembling lakes. Many side roads were reported as being underwater including Otis Stone Hunter Road, Hardgrove Grade (west of the Florida East Coast railroad tracks).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions during the month of March, in addition to very dry conditions since the beginning of the dry season, led to the development of severe drought conditions across Southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across most of South Florida during March. Most areas received less than 2 inches of rain, with some interior and western areas receiving less than 1 inch for the entire month. Some east coast areas received higher amounts from a stalled front during the middle of the month, with isolated areas seeing as much as 6 to 8 inches of rain.||Most of South Florida was placed under severe drought (D2) conditions.||The level of Lake Okeechobee fell from 12.83 feet to 12.20 feet by the end of the month. The Keetch-Byram Drought Index reached extreme levels across most of South Florida, indicative of very high fire danger values. As a result, several small brush fires were reported.

Thunderstorm winds downed trees and damaged a school and nearby home in Sneads.  Golf ball sized hail fell near New Hope in Holmes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst damaged the Sneads Elementary School and an adjacent home.  A 30-foot strip of metal below the eaves of one of the school's buildings was sheared off.  A nearby tree was uprooted.  Limbs and tops of trees on the school campus and surrounding woods were snapped.  A portion of the nearby home's roof was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on January 20th. High pressure of arctic origin settled over the region behind the cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to much of South Florida on the mornings of January 21 and 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over most of interior South Florida on the morning of January 22. Temperatures bottomed out as low as the mid 20s over portions of Glades, Hendry and Collier counties where a hard freeze was noted, with a low of 22 recorded at Palmdale in Glades County. Readings in the upper 20s to around 30 were observed over inland sections of Palm Beach county, with near freezing temperatures of 30-32 degrees over inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties. In addition to the freezing temperatures, widespread heavy frost formed over most of interior and northern sections of South Florida.||Crop damage was extensive in some areas, with total losses to bean and corn crops in parts of western Palm Beach county. Losses to tomato and strawberry crops were also noted in Hendry and Collier counties, with the exact extent of damage not yet known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The driest winter on record in many locations triggered the onset of severe drought conditions over southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driest winter on record over many locations in southeast Florida led to the onset of severe drought (D2) conditions. At Miami International Airport, winter season rainfall was only 0.74 inches, making it the driest winter on record. At Fort Lauderdale, winter season rainfall was only 0.39 inches, also the driest on record. At West Palm Beach, the winter season rainfall was 2.01 inches, the second driest on record.||No significant impacts had been noted as of the end of February due to the early stage of the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A arctic cold front swept through South Florida early on February 3. Cold air of arctic origin poured across the region in the wake of the front, causing freezing temperatures and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold air settled over Florida behind an arctic cold front which moved through the state on February 3. The coldest temperatures in 6 years were recorded at many locations. Low temperatures dropped into the mid to upper 20s over interior areas west and southwest of Lake Okeechobee, with temperatures of 32 degrees or lower over most of the remainder of south Florida. The only areas which did not see temperatures drop to freezing were coastal Broward and coastal and metro Miami-Dade counties. The lowest recorded temperature in south Florida was Palmdale which recorded a low of 22 degrees at a height of 2 feet.||Rather strong winds accompanied the cold overnight temperatures and led to early morning wind chills in the 20 to 30 degree range over all of South Florida.||Record lows for the date were tied at West Palm Beach (33 degrees) and Miami Beach (37 degrees).||The cold temperatures led to the death of a homeless man in Pompano Beach. Crops were damaged by the cold, including tropical fruits, tomatoes, corn and sugar cane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area and ushered in a cold and dry high pressure system. Freezing temperatures were felt across the nature coast and in normally colder pockets across inland areas further south. A few record lows were also broken.||Levy County: The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 7 hours and recorded a low of 25 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 26 degrees set in 1958.||Citrus County: The cooperative observer near Inverness was below freezing for 9 hours and recorded a low of 27 degrees. ||Sumter County: The cooperative observer near Bushnell recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Hernando County: The FAWN station near Brooksville and the ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport both recorded a low of 25 degrees and were below freezing for 8 hours. ||Inland Hillsborough County: The FAWN station at Dover recorded a low of 31 degrees, and the AWOS station near Vandenburg recorded a low of 32 degrees. Both locations were at or below freezing for 4 hours. ||Hardee County: The FAWN station near Ona recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Highlands County: The cooperative observer near Avon Park recorded a low of 26 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 32 degrees set in 1977.||Charlotte County: The cooperative observer near Punta Gorda recorded a new record low of 34 degrees, breaking the previous record low of 36 degrees set in 1977.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 7 hours and recorded a low of 25 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 26 degrees set in 1958.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on January 20th. High pressure of arctic origin settled over the region behind the cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to much of South Florida on the mornings of January 21 and 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over most of interior South Florida on the morning of January 22. Temperatures bottomed out as low as the mid 20s over portions of Glades, Hendry and Collier counties where a hard freeze was noted, with a low of 22 recorded at Palmdale in Glades County. Readings in the upper 20s to around 30 were observed over inland sections of Palm Beach county, with near freezing temperatures of 30-32 degrees over inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties. In addition to the freezing temperatures, widespread heavy frost formed over most of interior and northern sections of South Florida.||Crop damage was extensive in some areas, with total losses to bean and corn crops in parts of western Palm Beach county. Losses to tomato and strawberry crops were also noted in Hendry and Collier counties, with the exact extent of damage not yet known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The driest winter on record in many locations triggered the onset of severe drought conditions over southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driest winter on record over many locations in southeast Florida led to the onset of severe drought (D2) conditions. At Miami International Airport, winter season rainfall was only 0.74 inches, making it the driest winter on record. At Fort Lauderdale, winter season rainfall was only 0.39 inches, also the driest on record. At West Palm Beach, the winter season rainfall was 2.01 inches, the second driest on record.||No significant impacts had been noted as of the end of February due to the early stage of the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An already cold airmass was reinforced by even colder air behind a strong cold front. Low wind chills, freezing temperatures and frost affect most of east central Florida for three consecutive nights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures occurred for three consecutive nights. Wind chill readings in the 20s also occurred during the morning of the 21st. The coldest morning at most locations was on the 22nd, when hard freeze conditions spread south across many locations of the interior peninsula to Lake Okeechobee. Official temperature sites reached the mid to upper 20s, with a NWS Cooperative sites at Scottsmoor in NW Brevard county reaching 22 and Plymouth in NW Orange county reaching 16. Sub-freezing minimum readings and widespread frost occurred on the 23rd. Only the barrier islands south of Cape Canaveral remained at or slightly above freezing throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface west winds ahead of a rare late June cold front over north Florida along with strong high pressure aloft led to record heat over southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually high temperatures affected interior and eastern sections of South Florida on Sunday June 21 and Monday June 22. High temperatures reached the upper 90s in a few locations on June 21.||The hottest day was June 22, in which an all-time record high was tied at Fort Lauderdale at 100 degrees. Many locations over the southeast Florida metro area reached the upper 90s, falling just short of the 100 degree mark. Miami International Airport tied an all-time record for June with a reading of 98 degrees, and West Palm Beach broke a daily record with a high of 96. Moore Haven in Glades County reached 100 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually high temperatures affected interior and eastern sections of South Florida on Sunday June 21 and Monday June 22. High temperatures reached the upper 90s in a few locations on June 21.||The hottest day was June 22, in which an all-time record high was tied at Fort Lauderdale at 100 degrees. Many locations over the southeast Florida metro area reached the upper 90s, falling just short of the 100 degree mark. Miami International Airport tied an all-time record for June with a reading of 98 degrees, and West Palm Beach broke a daily record with a high of 96. Moore Haven in Glades County reached 100 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second mid/upper level trough moved down the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours of June 23. This triggered a second round of severe and damaging thunderstorms over much of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel size hail at US 1 and Atlantic Boulevard in Pompano Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second mid/upper level trough moved down the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours of June 23. This triggered a second round of severe and damaging thunderstorms over much of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large roof panels were ripped off the roof off a 21-story office building in downtown Fort Lauderdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. A very moist airmass and slow storm motion created a high heavy rainfall potential from afternoon and evening showers and storms that developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clay Electric Company Line Supervisor reported widespread power line damage due to thunderstorm winds across north and northeast portions of Alachua county. Lines were blown down along County Road 235 and County Road 231.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. Several days of heavy rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported a 20 foot diameter sinkhole developed on a private residence driveway near the intersection of Shiloh Road and Randolph Street. A car fell into the hole. The depth of the sinkhole was about 10 feet. No injuries were reported. Radar rainfall estimates ranged from 3 to 5 inches in the area over the past 24 hours.  A tow truck was trying to retrieve the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. Several days of heavy rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 5.50 inches in High Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. Several days of heavy rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured 4.68 inches of rainfall in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS crossed over southeast Georgia during the morning. The remnant upper level short wave trough crossed over northeast Florida during the afternoon which destabilized the atmosphere significantly to yield numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening.  The modified KJAX 12Z sounding indicated surface based lifted indices of -8 and CAPES of 4200 J/kg were possible during maximum heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along the east coast sea breeze boundary progressed inland and intensified to produce minor wind damage and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of downed tree limbs were observed along State Road 46 shortly after an intense thunderstorm passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a Jupiter man in the head while he was loading his truck to go to work.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Jupiter Heights resident loaded his truck as he prepared to go to work about 7AM. The man was then struck in the head and thrown about 10 feet. The bolt also burned an egg shaped hole in his hat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A possible funnel cloud was reported over western Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typical summer sea breeze thunderstorm activity was enhanced by strong instability to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms across southwest Florida.

A surface trough was in place across central Florida with an upper trough over the region moving upper disturbances over the area allowing for numerous showers and thunderstorms. A widespread 4 to 8 inches of rain fell across the Tampa Bay area resulting in some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee measured 6.81 inches of rain over the past eight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed over the St. Johns River north of the Buckman Bridge. The waterspout followed the river generally northward to northeast of the Ortega River entrance and south of the Fuller Warren Bridge near downtown Jacksonville before dissipating. Per eyewitness accounts and live TV web-cam coverage, there may have been two separate waterspouts near the Ortega River entrance. ||A severe storm over St. Johns county produced an outflow boundary which traveled north and collided with another out-flow boundary and the Atlantic coast sea breeze front. These three boundaries converged over the river which produced a small area of strong rotation which yielded the tornado.

This flooding occurred around the time of high tide since the 19th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glades County Emergency Management relayed report that a transformer blew out in Buckhead Ridge. Winds of 60 mph winds were reported in the area at that time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second mid/upper level trough moved down the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours of June 23. This triggered a second round of severe and damaging thunderstorms over much of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Belle Glade Public Library.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike injured two children who were standing under a tree. Both children were transported to a local hospital with non-life threatening injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning bolt struck a tree and injured two children that were standing beneath it. The 14 year old male and 12 year old female were both transported to a local hospital with non life threatening injuries. The female was still on the ground when emergency personnel arrived and had burn injuries consistent with a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from an intense thunderstorm started a fire with burned a church to the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries collided across western Volusia County and resulted in a line of intense thunderstorms with frequent to excessive cloud to ground lightning. Lightning from one of the storms along the line hit a church and started a fire which burned the church to the ground, resulting in a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced a very short duration of penny sized hail in western Port St. Lucie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter, west of Interstate 95 in Port St. Lucie observed a very short duration of penny sized hail and estimated winds gusts to around 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colliding sea breeze boundaries produced a narrow line of intense storms from southwest Volusia County to southwest Orange County. Reports of penny to nickel sized hail were received from Volusia and Orange Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter observed penny sized hail occurring near the intersection of University Boulevard and Golden Rod Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Florida man drowned during a prolonged episode of rough surf and rip currents. Life Guards observed rip currents along the beach and classified the death as a rip current fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rough surf and rip currents impacted the coast. A 47 year old Kissimmee, Florida man died in the surf. The county medical examiner ruled the death a drowning and life guards attributed the drowning to rip currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow and elevated surf created a higher than normal risk of rip currents at area beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards reported 7 rip current rescues with surf about 2 to 3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front was stretched across north Florida with a vorticity maximum moving east across the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Low level onshore flow also fueled some isolated heavy rains across coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 3.5 inches of rainfall since 2 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning thunderstorm caused minor wind damage and flooding across northern Walton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down near Argyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning thunderstorm caused minor wind damage and flooding across northern Walton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over six inches of water covered County Road 1883 just east of State Road 83.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded at South Florida Water Management District site STA5WX.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded at Landshark Stadium.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved south along the Georgia coast during the late afternoon and evening over an unstable airmass. Destabilization was enhanced by a passing upper level short wave trough and additional low level lift supported occurred due to the Atlantic sea breeze front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across central St. Johns county, including one at County Road 214 near Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface west winds ahead of a rare late June cold front over north Florida along with strong high pressure aloft led to record heat over southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually high temperatures affected interior and eastern sections of South Florida on Sunday June 21 and Monday June 22. High temperatures reached the upper 90s in a few locations on June 21.||The hottest day was June 22, in which an all-time record high was tied at Fort Lauderdale at 100 degrees. Many locations over the southeast Florida metro area reached the upper 90s, falling just short of the 100 degree mark. Miami International Airport tied an all-time record for June with a reading of 98 degrees, and West Palm Beach broke a daily record with a high of 96. Moore Haven in Glades County reached 100 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. A very moist airmass and slow storm motion created a high heavy rainfall potential from afternoon and evening showers and storms that developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 2.42 inches of rainfall in one hour (from 9-10 pm EDT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was positioned across north-central Florida with a low level northeast flow over the area. Southwest flow aloft steered a series of short wave troughs over the area which had deep layer moisture in place. A heavy rainfall producing convergent band developed along the northeast Florida coast which brought heavy rainfall to Duval and Flagler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The creek briefly came out of its banks and flooded backyards, however, the water did not flood West 33rd Street nor any homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area was positioned between high pressure over southern Florida and a cut-off low over the lower Mississippi River Valley and a moist southwest flow in the mid and upper levels. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down at a school just east of Tallahassee on the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado briefly touched down at the Apalachee Elementary School.  It mangled the metal roof of the main building.  The damage was confined to a small area about 100 yards long by 25 yards wide.  Maximum winds were estimated at 65 to 70 mph, which was consistent with EF0 tornado damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS crossed over southeast Georgia during the morning. The remnant upper level short wave trough crossed over northeast Florida during the afternoon which destabilized the atmosphere significantly to yield numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening.  The modified KJAX 12Z sounding indicated surface based lifted indices of -8 and CAPES of 4200 J/kg were possible during maximum heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the road along the Highway 312.

One of the men was 22 years old and the other's age was unclear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorms centered approximately 8 miles northwest of Key West produced a cloud-to-ground lightning strike, injuring one person near the south shore of Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a palm tree near the entrance to White Street Pier in Key West.  The bolt passed from the tree to a 19-year-old male.  The strike survivor remained conscious and was treated at Lower Keys Medical Center for minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid and upper level dry air was in place with a deep northerly flow over the local area. An MCS move across the area during the late afternoon and early evening which spawned scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home near the corner of Dunn Avenue and U.S. Highway 1. Extensive structural damage occurred. The cost of damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Ratliff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level ridge axis was over the Florida Panhandle with northwest steering flow over the local area. A sea breeze collision was anticipated along the Interstate 75 corridor. Mid level temperatures were near -10 C with PWAT values near 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage occurred in Obrien near 22205 129th Place. A hay barn rood was damaged and a cow shelter was destroyed. Post event review indicated an outflow boundary moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area and ushered in a cold and dry high pressure system. Freezing temperatures were felt across the nature coast and in normally colder pockets across inland areas further south. A few record lows were also broken.||Levy County: The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 7 hours and recorded a low of 25 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 26 degrees set in 1958.||Citrus County: The cooperative observer near Inverness was below freezing for 9 hours and recorded a low of 27 degrees. ||Sumter County: The cooperative observer near Bushnell recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Hernando County: The FAWN station near Brooksville and the ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport both recorded a low of 25 degrees and were below freezing for 8 hours. ||Inland Hillsborough County: The FAWN station at Dover recorded a low of 31 degrees, and the AWOS station near Vandenburg recorded a low of 32 degrees. Both locations were at or below freezing for 4 hours. ||Hardee County: The FAWN station near Ona recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Highlands County: The cooperative observer near Avon Park recorded a low of 26 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 32 degrees set in 1977.||Charlotte County: The cooperative observer near Punta Gorda recorded a new record low of 34 degrees, breaking the previous record low of 36 degrees set in 1977.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer near Avon Park recorded a low of 26 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 32 degrees set in 1977.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A arctic cold front swept through South Florida early on February 3. Cold air of arctic origin poured across the region in the wake of the front, causing freezing temperatures and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold air settled over Florida behind an arctic cold front which moved through the state on February 3. The coldest temperatures in 6 years were recorded at many locations. Low temperatures dropped into the mid to upper 20s over interior areas west and southwest of Lake Okeechobee, with temperatures of 32 degrees or lower over most of the remainder of south Florida. The only areas which did not see temperatures drop to freezing were coastal Broward and coastal and metro Miami-Dade counties. The lowest recorded temperature in south Florida was Palmdale which recorded a low of 22 degrees at a height of 2 feet.||Rather strong winds accompanied the cold overnight temperatures and led to early morning wind chills in the 20 to 30 degree range over all of South Florida.||Record lows for the date were tied at West Palm Beach (33 degrees) and Miami Beach (37 degrees).||The cold temperatures led to the death of a homeless man in Pompano Beach. Crops were damaged by the cold, including tropical fruits, tomatoes, corn and sugar cane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced 61 knot winds as it passed over the Ft. Pierce Airport (KFPR). A funnel cloud was also observed inland over St. Lucie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust to 61 knots reported by Ft. Pierce Airport ASOS (KFPR).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two airline employees were injured by a lightning strike while loading baggage onto an aircraft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two AirTran employees were injured by a lightning strike at Orlando International Airport. A bolt of lightning hit near a ramp while workers were loading bags onto a flight. Both workers were transported to area hospitals and were expected to recover. It appears the lightning strike eminated from the anvil of a cluster of storms centered 15 to 20 miles southeast of the airport. The time of the event is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms developed as the west coast sea breeze collided with an outflow boundary west of Orlando. An isolated, pulse severe storm developed and damaged several trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down near the intersection of Crystal Street and Rowe Avenue in Gotha.

The lowest recorded temperature in south Florida was Palmdale which recorded a low of 22 degrees at a height of 2 feet.||Rather strong winds accompanied the cold overnight temperatures and led to early morning wind chills in the 20 to 30 degree range over all of South Florida.||Record lows for the date were tied at West Palm Beach (33 degrees) and Miami Beach (37 degrees).||The cold temperatures led to the death of a homeless man in Pompano Beach. Crops were damaged by the cold, including tropical fruits, tomatoes, corn and sugar cane.

Crops were damaged by the cold, including tropical fruits, tomatoes, corn and sugar cane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on January 20th. High pressure of arctic origin settled over the region behind the cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to much of South Florida on the mornings of January 21 and 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over most of interior South Florida on the morning of January 22. Temperatures bottomed out as low as the mid 20s over portions of Glades, Hendry and Collier counties where a hard freeze was noted, with a low of 22 recorded at Palmdale in Glades County. Readings in the upper 20s to around 30 were observed over inland sections of Palm Beach county, with near freezing temperatures of 30-32 degrees over inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties. In addition to the freezing temperatures, widespread heavy frost formed over most of interior and northern sections of South Florida.||Crop damage was extensive in some areas, with total losses to bean and corn crops in parts of western Palm Beach county. Losses to tomato and strawberry crops were also noted in Hendry and Collier counties, with the exact extent of damage not yet known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area and ushered in a cold and dry high pressure system. Freezing temperatures were felt across the nature coast and in normally colder pockets across inland areas further south. A few record lows were also broken.||Levy County: The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 7 hours and recorded a low of 25 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 26 degrees set in 1958.||Citrus County: The cooperative observer near Inverness was below freezing for 9 hours and recorded a low of 27 degrees. ||Sumter County: The cooperative observer near Bushnell recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Hernando County: The FAWN station near Brooksville and the ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport both recorded a low of 25 degrees and were below freezing for 8 hours. ||Inland Hillsborough County: The FAWN station at Dover recorded a low of 31 degrees, and the AWOS station near Vandenburg recorded a low of 32 degrees. Both locations were at or below freezing for 4 hours. ||Hardee County: The FAWN station near Ona recorded a low of 29 degrees. ||Highlands County: The cooperative observer near Avon Park recorded a low of 26 degrees. This low broke the previous record low of 32 degrees set in 1977.||Charlotte County: The cooperative observer near Punta Gorda recorded a new record low of 34 degrees, breaking the previous record low of 36 degrees set in 1977.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The FAWN station near Brooksville and the ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport both recorded a low of 25 degrees and were below freezing for 8 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 66 mph at mesonet site cfsw in Clewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flower pots and traffic cones were blown down and some into the canal, and small palms were also blown down. Wind gusts were estimated at 60 to 70 mph at the east toll plaza on Alligator Alley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 62 mph at the Gator Run High School in Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 58 mph at the Cypress Bay High School in Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts from a thunderstorm knocked over a 200 pound gazebo and moved it 5 to 6 feet off its original setting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast produce damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS recorded a wind gust of 59 MPH with a thunderstorm at the Opa Locka Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of the wet season in South Florida produce more lightning damage along with hail and high wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel to dime sized hail accompanied with 50 mph wind gusts and over 1 inch of rain in 1/2 hour at Jupiter Farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of the wet season in South Florida produce more lightning damage along with hail and high wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site in the Everglades recorded a wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of the wet season in South Florida produce more lightning damage along with hail and high wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning knocked out power to near 1,600 residents of Palm Beach County after it knocked down power lines that cross Golf Street at Military Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of southwest Florida during the early evening hours.
Isolated afternoon thunderstorms produced a funnel cloud over Weston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of a funnel cloud were received over Weston. No touch down was indicated or observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms over the Gulf of Mexico produced outflows during the morning that converged with the east coast sea breeze during the early afternoon. A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and charred a fence.  The cost of damage was estimated and the timing of the event based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave across southwest Florida provided enhanced moisture and energy that enhanced widespread shower and thunderstorm activity across the area in the afternoon along both the east coast and west coast sea breezes. High moisture content and saturated soils elevated flooding concerns, and dry air advancing across the area from the east provided enough entrainment for a few strong wet downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at a gas station near Highways 21 and 26 in downtown Melrose estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph. Power outages were also reported in the area. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge extended from the western Gulf of Mexico to the Carolinas with a mid-latitude trough offshore of the New England coast. An inverted trough was offshore of the Bahamas to the Caribbean with the remnants of T.S. Ana near the Bahamas to Cuba. Tropical moisture and surface convergence increased over the area during the late afternoon which aided storm development near the seabreeze merger that occurred near the interstate 75 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Highway 129 near Interstate 75. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge extended from the western Gulf of Mexico to the Carolinas with a mid-latitude trough offshore of the New England coast. An inverted trough was offshore of the Bahamas to the Caribbean with the remnants of T.S. Ana near the Bahamas to Cuba. Tropical moisture and surface convergence increased over the area during the late afternoon which aided storm development near the seabreeze merger that occurred near the interstate 75 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across 74th Terrace on the north side of Live Oak. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge axis just shifted south of the area with a moist southwest flow over the area. A long wave trough was approaching from the central U.S. The sea breeze merger focused along the St. Johns River in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Country Road 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal convection developed over north Florida during the afternoon. Surface based CAPES were near 4000 J/kg with mid level shear around 40-50 kts in advance of the approaching front. Isolated severe storms developed in advance of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business owner along Race Track Road just west of Interstate 95 reported a brief period of penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of funnel clouds were sighted as convection moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager relayed a public report of a funnel cloud in the Jonesville area along State Road 26. A citizen reported a possible touchdown, but the report was unconfirmed and no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of funnel clouds were sighted as convection moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager relayed several reports of a funnel cloud in the Brooker vicinity along County Road 231. A few unconfirmed reports stated touchdown, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly flow and large swells enhanced the rip current risk along the Florida Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers were caught in a rip current at south Flagler Beach near the Flagler-Volusia county line. A bystander rescued both people as fire rescue arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail, high winds, and lightning affect metropolitan sections of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 inch hail in Jupiter with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a thunderstorm producing penny sized hail in Clewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms over the Gulf of Mexico produced outflows during the morning that converged with the east coast sea breeze during the early afternoon. A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various portions of Palm Coast had significant rainfall amounts. A spotter about 3 miles north of Palm Coast measured 5 inches of rainfall between 2 pm and 5 pm, and this spotter had a 2 day rainfall of 8 inches. The emergency management office measured 5.05 inches of rainfall between 2 pm and 4:52 pm. Street flooding was reported on Belle Terre Parkway, Palm Coast Parkway and Laramie Lane. Water was 6 inches deep in many areas along these roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms over the Gulf of Mexico produced outflows during the morning that converged with the east coast sea breeze during the early afternoon. A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 12 year old male near the Flagler Beach Pier. He was struck unconcious and partially paralyzed. He was air-lifted to a trama center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over Key West, and passed south into the nearshore Atlantic Waters. Gale force wind gusts and street flooding were observed at Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding to a depth of  two feet was observed at the corner of White and Eaton Streets in Key West, with a NWS employee reporting water one foot deep at the intersection of Thompson and Seminary Streets.  2.22 inches of rain was measured at the NWS Key West office from 09:45 AM to 10:45 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was ridging across southern Florida with a stalling frontal boundary settling into northern Florida. This put southwest wind flow in place across the area keeping a warm, moist and unstable environment over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near the intersection of Cross Creek Boulevard and Morris Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was in place across northern Florida with high pressure ridging across south Florida. This kept west to southwest wind flow across the local area and allowed for rip currents to develop at area beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32-year-old male was caught in a rip current off of Redington Shores Beach. It pulled him out to sea where he was caught treading water for over five hours. The Coast Guard rescued him and he was hospitalized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed as an outflow boundary from earlier thunderstorms collided with a remnant cumulus line over Big Pine Key. Gale force winds resulted in minor damage to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two dead pine trees were blown down, one across Palm Beach Road in central Big Pine Key.  A six-inch diameter tree limb from a healthy buttonwood tree was also blown down.  A nearby residence and CWOP automated station measured a wind gust out of the north-northwest at 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between high pressure off the southeast United States coast and Hurricane Ida over the northwestern Caribbean Sea and the southeast Gulf of Mexico produced strong winds over south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds of 25-30 mph persisted for over 2 days, with frequent gusts of 35-40 mph. Pompano Beach recorded a peak wind gust of 47 mph at 8 AM on November 9th. Other peak gusts include Virginia Key at 45 mph, Opa-Locka Airport at 40 mph and Palm Beach International Airport and Miami International Airport at 39 mph.||These winds produced power outages to approximately 3,000 customers across Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties. Some trees were reported to have toppled and/or uprooted, with the only confirmed report being a snapped pine tree which fell onto a car in Dania Beach, producing damage. Moderate beach erosion was also reported along some southeast Florida beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid to upper level disturbances moved across Florida from the Gulf of Mexico ahead of a frontal system over the central United States, causing periods of very heavy rainfall and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four apartments were flooded in an apartment building at 17255 SW 95 Ave in Palmetto Bay. Heavy early morning rain caused the roof of the building to collapse, sending water into the units.||Rainfall amounts in the area were generally in the 3 to 4 inch range through the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged a carport roof northwest of Bethel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A carport roof was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A homeless man died from hypothermia while sleeping outside at a campsite in Tallahassee early on the morning of the 22nd when temperatures were in the upper teens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A homeless man died from hypothermia while sleeping outside at a campsite in Tallahassee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An already cold airmass was reinforced by even colder air behind a strong cold front. Low wind chills, freezing temperatures and frost affect most of east central Florida for three consecutive nights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures occurred for three consecutive nights. Wind chill readings in the 20s also occurred during the morning of the 21st. The coldest morning at most locations was on the 22nd, when hard freeze conditions spread south across many locations of the interior peninsula to Lake Okeechobee. Official temperature sites reached the mid to upper 20s, with a NWS Cooperative sites at Scottsmoor in NW Brevard county reaching 22 and Plymouth in NW Orange county reaching 16. Sub-freezing minimum readings and widespread frost occurred on the 23rd. Only the barrier islands south of Cape Canaveral remained at or slightly above freezing throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across northern Florida with a trailing cold front that moved through the area behind it. Along and ahead of the cold front were numerous showers with a few embedded thunderstorms. A brief and weak tornado spawned from one thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow tornado briefly touched down 6.8 miles west of Wauchula at the Florida Institute for Neurologic Rehabilitation.  The tornado flipped a vehicle, blew out windows in multiple vehicles, caused roof damage to two buildings, and downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening cold front moved through the area with a strong squall line producing some thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained SKYWARN Spotter measured a wind gust of 60mph with an anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCS accompanied by a weakening shortwave trough provided enough destabilization to support severe storms across the local area.
A remnant MCS accompanied by a weakening shortwave trough provided enough destabilization to support severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured sustained speeds of 50 mph for one minute with a gust to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCS accompanied by a weakening shortwave trough provided enough destabilization to support severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCS accompanied by a weakening shortwave trough provided enough destabilization to support severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds destroyed a large Taco Bell sign located near Old St. Augustine Road and Interstate 295. A street sign was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorms produced strong winds that damaged a historic building in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph damaged a historic building at Fort Pickens. The winds blew double doors and frames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough axis was northwest of the area. Southerly flow focused the sea breeze collision across inland Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horseshoe Farms reported dime to penny size hail with estimated wind gusts to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough axis was northwest of the area. Southerly flow focused the sea breeze collision across inland Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EOC measured 0.87 inches of rainfall in thirty minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough helped to destabilize the airmass over the local area during the late afternoon and evening. Precipitable water was over 2 inches and jet dynamics were such that the right-rear quadrant was over our Georgia zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused street flooding in downtown Bunnell along the east side of State Road 100 between Pine Street and Peach Street. The water was 6 inches deep along portions of Highway 1 and State Road 100, as well as along some side streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily rainfall amounts across southern, central and eastern Flagler county ranged generally from 1 to 4 inches. Four inches was measured in downtown Bunnell and at the county fairgrounds.

A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Riverside Avenue near St. Vincent's Hospital toward Interstate 95. The water was 8 inches deep in some areas. A forecaster a NAS Jacksonville measured 1.81 inches of rainfall in 20 minutes.  From 3:55 pm local time to 4:58 pm local time, NAS Jacksonville measured a total of 3.19 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was positioned across north-central Florida with a low level northeast flow over the area. Southwest flow aloft steered a series of short wave troughs over the area which had deep layer moisture in place. A heavy rainfall producing convergent band developed along the northeast Florida coast which brought heavy rainfall to Duval and Flagler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee measured 6 inches of rain since 8 pm EDT last evening. Of that total, 3.25 inches had fallen since 1040 am EDT that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface west winds ahead of a rare late June cold front over north Florida along with strong high pressure aloft led to record heat over southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually high temperatures affected interior and eastern sections of South Florida on Sunday June 21 and Monday June 22. High temperatures reached the upper 90s in a few locations on June 21.||The hottest day was June 22, in which an all-time record high was tied at Fort Lauderdale at 100 degrees. Many locations over the southeast Florida metro area reached the upper 90s, falling just short of the 100 degree mark. Miami International Airport tied an all-time record for June with a reading of 98 degrees, and West Palm Beach broke a daily record with a high of 96. Moore Haven in Glades County reached 100 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards reported breaker heights of at least 6 feet at Hanna Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal band of showers with strong straight line winds transited north Florida between 06:00 and 12:00 EST on Wednesday, 7 January 2009. Non-thunderstorm wind gusts between 42 and 58 mph were measured immediately attending and just behind the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Suwannee County Emergency Management received call from dispatch at approximately 0739 EST in reference to a possible tornado impact at 13935 24th Street, Live Oak, FLorida, off of County Road 795. The resident's reported that at approximately 0630 EST the wind along with rain started to pick up and then it took off the roof to the rear side home���s carport area. Some of the debris landed in the front yard splicing an electric line in 2 places, the weight from the debris caused an electric pole to lean to the left. A second portion of the carport roof landed about 90 yards past the house in the tree line. The Suwannee County Emergency Management Agency determined the event to be due to Straight Line Wind and not a tornado. There were no other signs of visible damage to the residence or property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended across the area from just inland of St. Simons Island southwestward to just north of the Florida-Georgia state line near Valdosta. A surface low was broadening and weakening as it approached the Florida panhandle. Just enough surface based instability and moderate dynamics created an isolated severe thunderstorm threat across the area during the afternoon. There was a marginal tornado threat near the warm front and an isolated severe thunderstorm threat south of the front across the Florida peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported power lines blown down which caused power outages along 156th Place Road in Weirsdale. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long track supercell thunderstorm produced wind damage west of Quincy and near Lake Jackson, and finally spawned an F0 tornado northwest of Tallahassee near Miller Landing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm crossed Lake Jackson and produced wind damage just southwest of Brill Point Road.  In this area, several large trees either uprooted or snapped along the southwest-facing aspect of the lake.  All of the trees that were uprooted or snapped fell in an east-northeast direction.  One home had minor wind damage to a porch with some lawn furniture from the rear to the front of the home.  Additional tree damage was observed on the northeast side of Brill Point Road, oriented in an east-northeast direction.  Damage was consistent with straight-line winds with speeds of 70 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long track supercell thunderstorm produced wind damage west of Quincy and near Lake Jackson, and finally spawned an F0 tornado northwest of Tallahassee near Miller Landing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along the northern end of Lake Jackson east of Brill Point Road.  It traveled east-northeast and produced damage just south of the 2100 block of Miller Landing Road.  One home lost a sizable portion of it shingles on the southward-facing aspect of the home.  Debris from the roof was carried northward between 50 and 100 feet.  Three large trees were snapped in the backyard of the home and falling in a northward direction.  With the damage pattern nearly perpendicular to the storm path, the damage was consistent with an F0 tornado with wind speeds up to 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water from the heavy rainfall on the evening of December 17th continued into the afternoon hours of December 18th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding occurred over downtown Miami, the Brickell District south of downtown, as well as the Allapattah section northwest of downtown. Several main streets were impassable as a result of the flooding, primarily along Brickell Drive. Numerous motorists were stranded by the high water and at least one business in the Allapattah area had water entering their structure.||Rainfall reports from the area were generally between 3 and 4 inches, with as much as 4.3 inches in downtown Miami.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving north across south Florida during the afternoon and evening hours of December 17th triggered a persistent band of heavy showers which produced severe flooding over sections of southeastern Broward County and northeastern Miami-Dade County. (NOTE: damage estimates for Broward County flood event contained in Flash Flood event).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive and severe flooding occurred over southeastern Broward and northeastern Miami-Dade counties. Hardest-hit communities were Dania Beach, Hollywood, Hallandale Beach, Pembroke Park and Aventura. As much as 14 inches of rain fell in about 6 hours in North Miami Beach, with other mesonet reports along with radar estimates showing an area of 8 to 13 inches of rain primarily along and east of I-95 in the above-mentioned communities. Severe flooding began around 730 PM in Dania Beach, gradually progressing southward between 8 and 10 PM to cover the rest of the affected area. ||A total of 101 homes sustained major flood damage in Broward County, with an additional 88 homes having minor damage. Water was reported to be as high as 2 feet deep inside some homes, and a mobile home park in Pembroke Park had to be evacuated due to the rising water. Shelters were opened in Hallandale Beach and Hollywood to accommodate the evacuees. Six businesses also sustained significant water damage. Two buildings had roof collapses, one in Hallandale Beach and another in Aventura. Hundreds of cars were damaged or destroyed by the water.||In addition to the water damage, about 2,000 customers lost power in Broward and Miami-Dade counties. Damage totals are estimated based partially on average amount of loss per home.

Many of the storms that occurred resulted in flooding of poor drainage areas, including some urban areas in Panama City and Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported.
