EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 13th, a series of severe thunderstorms produced numerous reports of wind damage and large hail across the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.  Many of the storms that occurred resulted in flooding of poor drainage areas, including some urban areas in Panama City and Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of marble to penny size hail on U.S. Highway 98 in Panama City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 13th, a series of severe thunderstorms produced numerous reports of wind damage and large hail across the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.  Many of the storms that occurred resulted in flooding of poor drainage areas, including some urban areas in Panama City and Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported south of Greensboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air in the wake of the heavy rains and severe flooding the previous night contributed to a very unstable atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving south across Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed report from Miami-Dade PD of a tornado in an unpopulated area east of the city of Homestead near SW 132 Avenue and 328 Street at around 11 AM. Subsequent site survey noted no visible damage, primarily due to lack of structures and objects in area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over the area behind a strong cold front and caused some record-breaking cold temperatures across the area. Freezing temperatures occurred for three straight mornings, with the first night being an advective freeze, and the second and third nights being radiational freezes. ||Levy County: The Bronson FAWN station was below freezing for 12 hours and was below 27 degrees for 6 hours.  The low temperature was 24 degrees.||Citrus County:  A home weather station in Citrus Springs recorded a low of 24 degrees. The Inverness cooperation station recorded a low of 28 degrees.||Sumter County:  The low temperature in The Villages was 26 degrees and the low in Bushnell was 27 degrees. ||Hernando County:  The cooperative station near Weeki Wachee reported a low of 22 degrees.||Pasco County:  The cooperative station at Saint Leo College reported a low of 29 degrees and a home weather station in Dade City recorded a low of 28 degrees. ||Hillsborough County:  A low temperature of 29 degrees was recorded at the Vandenburg airport and at the cooperative station near Dover and a home weather station in Plant City recorded a low of 28 degrees. ||Polk County:  The cooperative station near Lake Wales reported a low of 27 degrees.||Manatee County:  The cooperative station near Fort Green recorded a low of 31 degrees.||Hardee County:  The FAWN station near Ona recorded a low of 32 degrees and a home weather station in Zolfo Springs recorded 31 degrees. ||Highlands County:  The cooperative station near Avon Park recorded a low of 30 degrees and a home weather station in Archbold recorded 26 degrees. ||DeSoto County:  The FAWN station near Arcadia recorded a low of 29 degrees.

A pre-frontal squall line of storms moved across the forecast area during the pre-dawn hours of February 19th, and the line weakened after sunrise as the convection moved across our southern Florida zones. The storms caused scattered wind damage. Low level wind shear was near 60-70 knots with most-unstable CAPES around 500-1000 J/kg. Strong 850 mb winds of 30-40 knots were noted just behind the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A construction worker on Lem Turner Road reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of storms moved across the forecast area during the pre-dawn hours of February 19th, and the line weakened after sunrise as the convection moved across our southern Florida zones. The storms caused scattered wind damage. Low level wind shear was near 60-70 knots with most-unstable CAPES around 500-1000 J/kg. Strong 850 mb winds of 30-40 knots were noted just behind the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida Highway Patrol reported that a tree was blown down across the road 6884 Southwest State Road 751 in Jasper.

A pre-frontal squall line of storms moved across the forecast area during the pre-dawn hours of February 19th, and the line weakened after sunrise as the convection moved across our southern Florida zones. The storms caused scattered wind damage. Low level wind shear was near 60-70 knots with most-unstable CAPES around 500-1000 J/kg. Strong 850 mb winds of 30-40 knots were noted just behind the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Highway 90, east of Fallmouth. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of storms moved across the forecast area during the pre-dawn hours of February 19th, and the line weakened after sunrise as the convection moved across our southern Florida zones. The storms caused scattered wind damage. Low level wind shear was near 60-70 knots with most-unstable CAPES around 500-1000 J/kg. Strong 850 mb winds of 30-40 knots were noted just behind the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hamilton County Road Department Road Superintendent reported widespread tree damage reports across the area.  Trees were blown down across County Road 141, County Road 146, The Godwin Bridge Southeast at 95th Street and another tree was blown down across County Road 135.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked northeast along the Florida Keys.  The thunderstorm first produced a weak tornado at the extreme southeast tip of Key West and south Stock Island, then tracked northeast just north of Big Pine Key before crossing the Upper Florida Keys from the west-southwest at Key Largo.  A second weak tornado was reported at several Florida Bay shoreline properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado produced EF0 damage across the extreme southeast part of Key West through south Stock Island.  The tornado was first observed by a motorist just offshore South Roosevelt Boulevard before moving briefly onshore at a hotel.  Over a half dozen palm trees with some decay were snapped at the base.  Lawn furniture associated with the hotel's tiki hut north of the tornado path were blown southeast onto South Roosevelt Boulevard in the torando's wake.  The tornado tracked northeast across Cow Key Channel before moving ashore south Stock Island along 12th Avenue.  Two trees were downed onto 12th Avenue in a northeast direction.  A deck umbrella was lofted northeast from the east end of 11th Avenue onto power lines on 5th Street, bringing down the lines and causing a power outage throughout the area.  The tornado continued northeast and likely lifted or dissipated over 5th Avenue northeast of Bernstein Park.  There, a small ornamental tree was found snapped at the base and laying toward the west partially onto 5th Street.  Several trash cans placed around Bernstein park had fallen and rolled southward or southeastward.  At the tornado path northeast terminus, a television cable line was downed.  Maximum winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph along the path from Key West through 11th Avenue in Stock Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong to briefly severe storms moved east across the western Florida Panhandle during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along Highway 4 on the eastern portions of Munson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong to briefly severe storms moved east across the western Florida Panhandle during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Rosa 911 reported a garage/shed was damaged on J.H. Carr Road near Jay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked northeast along the Florida Keys.  The thunderstorm first produced a weak tornado at the extreme southeast tip of Key West and south Stock Island, then tracked northeast just north of Big Pine Key before crossing the Upper Florida Keys from the west-southwest at Key Largo.  A second weak tornado was reported at several Florida Bay shoreline properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS investigated a newspaper report of a tornado at Key Largo, and found the same supercell which spawned a weak tornado in the Lower Florida Keys also caused a short-lived weak tornado near Mile Marker 104 of the Overseas Highway.  The tornado produced minor damage with some outdoor furniture destroyed and ornamental palm trees downed in a parking lot at a Florida Bay-side business, and porch screens blown out at a restaurant next door.  Maximum winds estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to locally severe storms moved east across the western Florida Panhandle during the early evening hours on Christmas Eve, producing localized winds to near 60 mph and sporadic damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds produced damage to an apartment complex where the brick siding was torn off a building.  In the immediate area, trees and power lines were downed.  A 5 feet by 8 feet metal advertising sign was also destroyed nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to locally severe storms moved east across the western Florida Panhandle during the early evening hours on Christmas Eve, producing localized winds to near 60 mph and sporadic damage reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced high winds that damaged trees and power lines.  Heavy rain from the thunderstorms also produced flooding across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duke Field Fire department at Eglin Air Force base reported a tornado on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced high winds that damaged trees and power lines.  Heavy rain from the thunderstorms also produced flooding across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from thunderstorms flooded several roads around Crestview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved over the area behind a strong cold front and caused some record-breaking cold temperatures across the area. Freezing temperatures occurred for three straight mornings, with the first night being an advective freeze, and the second and third nights being radiational freezes. Temperatures in the 20s were felt as far south as Charlotte County and durations of sub-freezing temperatures were for several hours each night. ||Damage to crops varied widely depending on if fields had freeze protection equipment.  Strawberries and blueberries fared well if they had freeze protection.  Citrus growers had significant leaf loss, varying extents of wood damage, some fruit loss, and reduction of yield due to internal damage.  Many ranch pastures were severely frosted and producers have had to rely on hay.  No monetary values were available as of March. ||Levy County:  The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 14 hours and reached 19 degrees. ||Citrus County:  The cooperative station near Inverness recorded a low of 20 degrees. ||Sumter County:  The cooperative observer near Bushnell recorded a low of 19 degrees while The Villages recorded a low of 27 degrees. ||Hernando County:  The FAWN station near Brooksvill recorded a low of 16 degrees while the cooperative observer near Brooksville on Chin Hill recorded a low of 30 degrees. ||Pasco County:  The cooperative observer at Saint Leo College recorded a low of 28 degrees. ||Hillsborough County:   The low at Vandenburg Airport was 24 degrees. ||Polk County:  The cooperative observer near Lake Wales recorded a low of 25 degrees. ||Manatee County:  The cooperative observer near Fort Green recorded a low of 23 degrees and was below freezing for 10 hours. ||Hardee County:  The Ona FAWN station recorded a low temperature of 25 degrees. ||Highlands County:  The low temperature in Archbold was 22 degrees. ||Sarasota County:  The FAWN station near North Port recorded a low temperature of 28 degrees. ||Desoto County:  The FAWN station near Arcadia recorded a low of 25 degrees. ||Charlotte County:  The ASOS in Punta Gorda recorded freezing temperatures for six hours with a low of 26 degrees. ||Lee County:  The ASOS at the Regional Southwest International Airport recorded a low temperature of 31 degrees.

An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on January 20th. High pressure of arctic origin settled over the region behind the cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to much of South Florida on the mornings of January 21 and 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over most of interior South Florida on the morning of January 22. Temperatures bottomed out as low as the mid 20s over portions of Glades, Hendry and Collier counties where a hard freeze was noted, with a low of 22 recorded at Palmdale in Glades County. Readings in the upper 20s to around 30 were observed over inland sections of Palm Beach county, with near freezing temperatures of 30-32 degrees over inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties. In addition to the freezing temperatures, widespread heavy frost formed over most of interior and northern sections of South Florida.||Crop damage was extensive in some areas, with total losses to bean and corn crops in parts of western Palm Beach county. Losses to tomato and strawberry crops were also noted in Hendry and Collier counties, with the exact extent of damage not yet known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved caused a weak line of storms that produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 MPH and caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small tree limbs broke and fell onto power lines causing power outages in the Fruitville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved caused a weak line of storms that produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 MPH and caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small tree limbs snapped, shopping carts were turned over and the awning of a grocery store was damaged in Sarasota. A woman pushing a shopping cart and holding an umbrella was knocked down while walking to her car. She was transported to the hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over the area behind a strong cold front and caused several days of freezing temperatures across the area. Winds were near calm for the second night allowing for a radiational freeze across much of the area. A strong northerly wind caused temperatures in the 20s to advect as far south as Sarasota, Desoto, and Highlands counties. Winds were near calm for the second night allowing for a radiational freeze across much of the area. Light easterly flow the third night allowed temperatures to moderate at most locations, with the nature coast still experiencing freezing temperatures. ||Damage to crops varied widely depending on if fields had freeze protection equipment. Strawberries, blueberries, and citrus fared well if they had freeze protection. No monetary values were available as of April. |||Levy County: The cooperative observer near Chiefland was below freezing for 12 hours on the 5th and 6th, reaching a low of 19 degrees on the 5th and 18 degrees on the 6th. ||Sumter County: The cooperative observer near Bushnell was below freezing for 8 hours on the 5th and 6th, recording a low of 24 degrees on the 5th and 21 degrees on the 6th.||Citrus County: The cooperative station near Inverness recorded a low of 25 degrees on the 5th and 20 degrees on the 6th.||Hernando County: The FAWN station near Brooksville was below freezing for an average of 11 hours on the 5th and 6th, recording a low of 23 degrees on the 5th and 18 degrees on the 6th.||Pasco County: The cooperative observer at Saint Leo College recorded a low of 27 degrees on the 5th and 30 degrees on the 6th. ||Hillsborough County: The Vandenburg Airport was below freezing for an average of 9 hours on the 5th and 6th, recording a low of 28 degrees on the 5th and 27 degrees on the 6th.||Polk County: The cooperative station at the Lakeland Linder Airport recorded a low of 27 degrees on the 5th and 30 degrees on the 6th. ||Manatee County: The cooperative station near Duette recorded a low of 27 degrees on the 5th and 26 degrees on the 6th.||Sarasota County: The cooperative station near Venice recorded a low of 29 degrees on the 5th. The FAWN station near North Port recorded a low of 27 degrees on the 6th. ||Hardee County: The FAWN station near Ona was below freezing for 6 hours on the 5th and recorded a low of 26 degrees. The low was 31 degrees on the 6th. ||Desoto County: The FAWN station near Arcadia was below freezing for an average of 5 hours on the 5th and the 6th, reaching a low of 26 degrees on the 5th and 31 degrees on the 6th. ||Highlands County: The cooperative station near Avon Park recorded a low of 28 degrees on the 5th and 32 degrees on the 6th. ||Charlotte County: The ASOS station in Punta Gorda was below freezing for 3 hours on the 5th and the 6th, reaching a low of 30 degrees on the 5th and 31 degrees on the 6th.  ||Lee County: The ASOS station at the Regional Southwest International Airport recorded a low of 31 degrees on the 5th. Freezing temperatures were not felt across the county on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative station near Inverness recorded a low of 20 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vandenburg Airport AWOS was below freezing for 10 hours, recording a low of 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved over the area behind a strong cold front and caused some record-breaking cold temperatures across the area. Freezing temperatures occurred for three straight mornings, with the first night being an advective freeze, and the second and third nights being radiational freezes. Temperatures in the 20s were felt as far south as Charlotte County and durations of sub-freezing temperatures were for several hours each night. ||Damage to crops varied widely depending on if fields had freeze protection equipment.  Strawberries and blueberries fared well if they had freeze protection.  Citrus growers had significant leaf loss, varying extents of wood damage, some fruit loss, and reduction of yield due to internal damage.  Many ranch pastures were severely frosted and producers have had to rely on hay. A cold front caused a line of showers and thunderstorms that produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 MPH as it moved through the Nature Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off of a small tool shed in Chiefland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm in advance of an eastward-moving line of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across Vaca Key and the city of Marathon. Isolated wind damage and measured severe thunderstorm wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 52 knots (60 mph) was observed at the Florida Keys Marathon Airport in association with a severe thunderstorm.  One tree was uprooted near the ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of the 18th, a severe thunderstorm entered Walton County, which produced baseball sized hail and damaging winds near Darlington.  During the predawn hours of the 19th, a severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Madison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball sized hail was reported between Glendale and Darlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought large hail to northwest Florida.
A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flagler Beach Fire & Rescue reported a 40 mph wind gust with numerous branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bradford County Emergency Management Agency reported several sinkholes had developed across the county due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marion County Emergency Management reported a sinkhole developed north of Dunnellon due to heavy rainfall over the past day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported drainage basins and streams near bank full countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported roads flooded and closed near County Road 143 near Jennings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jacksonville International Airport ASOS officially reported 3.05 inches of rain between 20:00 and 23:00 EST (21:00-24:00 EDT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was down on the Jacksonville north side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Funnel cloud was reported along County Road 136 near Falmouth in the northwest portion of Suwannee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported three quarter inch sized hail along County Road 25a near U.Ss. Highways 441 and 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported three quarter inch sized hail just northwest of Jacksonville International Airport  near Lem Turner Road in Nassau County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under warm and dry high pressure, a strong dust devil developed and tore an aluminum overhang off of a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An aluminum overhang was torn off of a mobile home in Ruskin. There was flat cumulus and interviews with two eye witnesses both said there was rotation at the ground. It was determined this was a dust devil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with early season sea-breeze convection produced multiple strong storms and an isolated severe storm with hail and a brief tornado touchdown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public observed penny sized hail in Geneva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with early season sea-breeze convection produced multiple strong storms and an isolated severe storm with hail and a brief tornado touchdown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at a rural gas station observed a brief tornado touchdown approximately 5 miles to the southeast. This time and location was consistent with strong rotation evident on doppler radar. No damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down across road by winds. Road obstruction from downed tree and powerlines at E. Rose Ave. and Holly Ave. in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail fell at Timbertop Nursery in Fruitland Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A national weather service employee reported nickel sized hail just northeast of Jacksonville International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level impulses moved over a stationary frontal boundary across north Florida and southeast Georgia producing severe weather and flooding.  This event continued into April 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down as an EF-0 in an open field just east of the Suwannee River and south of the Progress Energy power plant. It increased to intensity becoming a low end EF-1 tornado several hundred feet down track where it impacted several farm buildings and mobile homes. Several small farm structures were destroyed and winds here we estimated to be between 86 and 90 mph. The tornado continued moving northeastward as an EF-0 snapping trees along its path near the entrance to the Suwannee River State Park and along Stagecoach Road. It then went through a wooded area emerging along West Tower Road where it began the occlusion process. The rear flank downdraft heavily damaged two chicken houses with a loss of about a quarter million dollars. At this point the tornado took a northward turn toward the Jenkins Farm house destroying a large work shed with heavy machinery and damaging the home. The tornado was rated as an EF-1 here with winds of 95 to 100 mph. Debris from the shed was strewn across the field north of the Jenkins Farm house to Stagecoach Road.

Wind blew tree into an apartment building in the Hidden Village Apartment complex one half mile south of Archer Road.  Trees were also blown down on a home at 6632 SW 81st Street in Gainesville. Heavy rain and power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A arctic cold front swept through South Florida early on February 3. Cold air of arctic origin poured across the region in the wake of the front, causing freezing temperatures and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49-year-old homeless man died of hypothermia as a result of exposure to temperatures in the 30s and wind chill reading as low as the mid to upper 20s. The man had settled for the night in a tent near a wooded area off Powerline Road in Pompano Beach. ||Witnesses told investigators the man had consumed an excessive amount of alcohol the previous evening. The medical examiner indicated hypothermia as a very important element, along with the amount of alcohol in the man's body.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on January 20th. High pressure of arctic origin settled over the region behind the cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to much of South Florida on the mornings of January 21 and 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over most of interior South Florida on the morning of January 22. Temperatures bottomed out as low as the mid 20s over portions of Glades, Hendry and Collier counties where a hard freeze was noted, with a low of 22 recorded at Palmdale in Glades County. Readings in the upper 20s to around 30 were observed over inland sections of Palm Beach county, with near freezing temperatures of 30-32 degrees over inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties. In addition to the freezing temperatures, widespread heavy frost formed over most of interior and northern sections of South Florida.||Crop damage was extensive in some areas, with total losses to bean and corn crops in parts of western Palm Beach county. Losses to tomato and strawberry crops were also noted in Hendry and Collier counties, with the exact extent of damage not yet known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across the Florida panhandle and produced wind damage and hail.
An extended dry spell led to drought conditions and several wildfires across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Springs 10 fire in the Ocala National Forest had consumed 10,022 acres as of the morning of 3/19/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moved across central Florida on the afternoon and evening of February 2nd. Thunderstorms associated with the low moved east across south Florida during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving across northern Palm Beach County sparked a fire which destroyed a mobile home in the Suni Sands Mobile Home Park in Jupiter. The owner's dog died in the fire, and the owners were out to dinner when the fire started.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across Southeast Florida led to the continuation of severe drought conditions across most of the area during the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across most of South Florida during March. Most areas received less than 2 inches of rain, with some interior and western areas receiving less than 1 inch for the entire month. Some east coast areas received higher amounts from a stalled front during the middle of the month, with isolated areas seeing as much as 6 to 8 inches of rain.||Most of South Florida was placed under severe drought (D2) conditions.||The level of Lake Okeechobee fell from 12.83 feet to 12.20 feet by the end of the month. The Keetch-Byram Drought Index reached extreme levels across most of South Florida, indicative of very high fire danger values. As a result, several small brush fires were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through South Florida during the day of March 31. Unstable air south of the front combined with warm temperatures combined to produced strong and severe thunderstorms over Palm Beach County. A total of about 5,000 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado touchdown occurred in West Palm Beach near Palm Beach Lakes Boulevard and Australian Avenue. This is the same storm that produced the tornado in Palm Beach Gardens, but eyewitness reports and photographs indicate a likely second tornado touchdown in the West Palm Beach area. Damage was minor (EF0) consisting of downed traffic signals, tree branches broken off, and a bus bench flipped over.

Minor roof damage was noted to a few residential buildings, as well as trees uprooted and a fence blown over near Palm Beach Gardens High School. Final tornado rating was EF0 based on Emergency Manager survey and analysis of damage photos.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia dropping heavy rainfall and producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof fire was ignited by lightning in the eagle Creek sub-division east of U.S. Highway 1. A woman and a young child escaped through a bedroom window after smoke alarms woke the family. Her husband and two other young children escaped through the front door.  A family dog was also rescued and treated with a pet oxygen mask after it was found inside the house. The worst damage from the fire was to the attic above the garage, though smoke penetrated the rest of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds associated with a meso-high occurred after passage of a squall-line containing several damaging thunderstorms, lasting from late afternoon until early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gradient wind gust to 58 mph at Melbourne International Airport (KMLB).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Storm Survey revealed short line of damage through several buildings comprising the middle of the Frontenac Flea Market. The top wooden cross member of a single telephone phone was broken adjacent to the flea market, on the eastern side of US1 on Canebreakers Drive. Estimated winds were 65-75 mph. Two eyewitnesses driving north on US1 just SSE of the flea market observed a white, rotating vortex hit the flea market and cross US1 in front of them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mile long swath of straight-line wind damage, estimated 55-65 mph produced light to moderate damage to several homes and one old residence with significant roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several businesses experienced minor structural damage consistent with straight-line winds of 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore southeasterly winds along the Palm Beach County coast led to the formation of rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20-year-old Boynton Beach resident drowned when caught in a rip current just north of Lake Worth Pier. The victim was swimming about 200 yards offshore. Two people attempted to rescue the victim to no avail. ||City lifeguards had already left for the day, and there had been warning flags posted throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms on the 2nd produced damaging winds, large hail, flooding and a tornado across much of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes were damaged on Mt Sinai Road just north of U.S. Highway 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a large wildfire in the Big Cypress Preserve in interior Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wildfire in the East Hinson Marsh area of Big Cypress National Preserve on April 22. The fire started one mile north of I-75 (Alligator Alley) and east of SR 29. Very dry conditions caused by the drought across the region, along with seasonably strong east winds, caused the wildfire to spread rapidly. The fire eventually spread across both sides of I-75 causing almost nightly road closures due to the thick smoke and stable nighttime conditions. SR 29 was also periodically closed due to thick smoke. As of the end of the month, the fire had burned 30,005 acres and was 85% contained. No structures were damaged due to the remote location of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the western Atlantic produced a strong easterly wind flow the weekend of April 25 and 26. This led to the development of strong and dangerous rip currents along the southeast Florida Atlantic beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 73-year-old Washington D.C. man visiting his condo in Pompano Beach drowned while attempting to rescue two boys who were caught in a rip current off 1000 South Ocean Boulevard in Pompano Beach. The man swam out to save the 2 boys, but was overwhelmed by the surf and rip currents and suffered a heart attack. A second man who attempted to rescue the victim was taken to the hospital for observation after having trouble breathing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the western Atlantic produced a strong easterly wind flow the weekend of April 25 and 26. This led to the development of strong and dangerous rip currents along the southeast Florida Atlantic beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24-year-old Cape Coral man drowned in rough surf and rip currents near Peruvian Avenue in Palm Beach, just south of the beach's guarded section. Lifeguards had just gone off-duty minutes before the drowning. Several people, including two of the victim's friends, tried to help.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter just northeast of Jacksonville International Airport reported nickel sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 40 mph or stronger reported during the morning with branches down. A part of a tree was down across a power line knocking out power for about 45 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on State Road 121 near Worthington Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gusts above 50 mph, with a peak gust of 71 mph,  was recorded on a private weather station. Five trees were snapped about 25 feet up the trees with the largest being 16 feet in diameter. A portion of the fencing was destroyed and they experienced a power outage of one and half hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter west of Starke reported trees down on State Roads 229 and 235, as well as at his residence 10 miles west of Starke. Emergency Management reported Bradford County had about 30 roads closed due to trees or power lines across the roadway. One tree fell on a house and they had 315 customers of Clay Electric without power and 188 customers of Florida Power & Light out of power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and a fence was damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat dock was damaged along with several small boats being submerged on on the east side of Round Lake.  A nearby home was damaged.  A citizen in the area reported a roaring sound with several trees uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down on Wilderness Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter/spring drought over South Florida intensified during early parts of May, but came to a quick end as copious rainfall fell during the last 2 weeks of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought which began during the late winter months continued into early and mid May over South Florida. A very dry start to the month prompted the issuance of extreme drought (D3) conditions over virtually all of South Florida by the middle of the month. ||The onset of the rainy season around May 11 brought copious rainfall to the region as a low pressure trough in the upper levels of the atmosphere set up near South Florida, effectively ending the drought by the last week of May. Many areas received in excess of 10 inches of rain for the month, virtually all of it falling in a period of only 2 weeks. West Palm Beach recorded its second wettest May on record with 15.69 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries collided over coastal Volusia County, resulting in the rapid development of a severe storm near Ormond Beach. This cell then back-built south-southwest along a boundary during the late afternoon, producing a complex of strong to severe cells farther inland. A waterspout developed within the initial severe cell offshore of Ormond Beach and moved onto the beach as a tornado, reaching the adjacent backyard of several homes just beyond the beach. This vortex, or more likely other vorticies associated with the same cell then meandered south along the beach and near-shore Atlantic waters for about ten minutes (about 3 minutes over or near the beach). Early in the evening, another tornado (rated EF0) developed inland over Port Orange and damaged over 150 manufactured/mobile homes before lifting. One person sustained minor injuries from broken glass. Also, between Ormond Beach and Port Orange, a man was struck and injured by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by lightning outside his Holly Hill home while gardening during the beginning of an approaching storm. The man was knocked unconscious for several minutes by the strike and awoke bleeding, and with a numb neck and right leg from the knee down. He also sustained burns inside his mouth and in his eyes. After gaining consciousness, he crawled into his house and his wife called 911. He was then rushed to a local hospital emergency room.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries collided over coastal Volusia County, resulting in the rapid development of a severe storm near Ormond Beach. This cell then back-built south-southwest along a boundary during the late afternoon, producing a complex of strong to severe cells farther inland. A waterspout developed within the initial severe cell offshore of Ormond Beach and moved onto the beach as a tornado, reaching the adjacent backyard of several homes just beyond the beach. This vortex, or more likely other vorticies associated with the same cell then meandered south along the beach and near-shore Atlantic waters for about ten minutes (about 3 minutes over or near the beach). Early in the evening, another tornado (rated EF0) developed inland over Port Orange and damaged over 150 manufactured/mobile homes before lifting. One person sustained minor injuries from broken glass. Also, between Ormond Beach and Port Orange, a man was struck and injured by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Beach Patrol witnessed a waterspout moving onshore the beach, then meandering southward along the beach, into backyards of some residences adjacent to beach and back into the near-shore Atlantic waters. The beach patrol indicated that multiple vortices formed, dissipated and re-formed along the indicated north-south track. The tornado tossed some light chairs and tables in a backyard just inland from the beach and also moved a small catamaran from the upper part of the beach to the surf-line and snapped the mast. Circular marks were evident in the beach sand where the tornado crossed from the backyards to the ocean, near the location of the deposited catamaran. The tornado was rated at the minimum end of the EF-0 range (65 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries collided over coastal Volusia County, resulting in the rapid development of a severe storm near Ormond Beach. This cell then back-built south-southwest along a boundary during the late afternoon, producing a complex of strong to severe cells farther inland. A waterspout developed within the initial severe cell offshore of Ormond Beach and moved onto the beach as a tornado, reaching the adjacent backyard of several homes just beyond the beach. This vortex, or more likely other vorticies associated with the same cell then meandered south along the beach and near-shore Atlantic waters for about ten minutes (about 3 minutes over or near the beach). Early in the evening, another tornado (rated EF0) developed inland over Port Orange and damaged over 150 manufactured/mobile homes before lifting. One person sustained minor injuries from broken glass. Also, between Ormond Beach and Port Orange, a man was struck and injured by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS ground survey of damage revealed a weak EF0 tornado touched down in the extreme northeast portion of the Lighthouse Point mobile home community. The tornado continued south-southwest and strengthened to a strong EF0 tornado within the Laurelwood manufactured home community, then lifted in the extreme northwest portion of the Brandy Hills subdivision. Light debris (insulation, styrofoam) was deposited up to 2 miles downstream (south-southeast) from where the tornado lifted. 154 homes sustained damage: 3 uninhabitable, 60 major damage, 43 minor damage, 48 very minimal damage. One man sustained a minor injury, due to cuts from broken glass. EF assessment: DI3, DOD4, LB-EXP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass existed over the area with a broad scale upper level trough over the region. Convection developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A REACT HAM operator estimated three inches of rainfall in 30 minutes. West central Palm Street flooded. The Flagler County Sheriffs office reported one foot of water on Belle Terrie Parkway in central Palm Coast. About 1.75 inches of rainfall was measured from a Grand Haven CERT member in Eastern Palm Coast within 45 minutes. About 2.8 inches of rainfall fell in 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced hail up to size of baseballs in and around Perry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported on West Glenn Street in Perry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north to south line of thunderstorms developed rapidly over central Orange County, including metro Orlando as the west coast sea breeze boundary intersected another mesoscale boundary. A funnel cloud associated with a strong storm tracked just south of Highway 528, then touched down briefly and heavily damaged the roof of a large warehouse. Excessive lightning, nearly 2200 cloud to ground strikes in an hour, affected central Orange County. Lightning struck a apartment complex north of Orlando and started a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an apartment building during a severe thunderstorm and started a fire. Nearly 2200 cloud to ground lightning strikes occurred across central Orange County in an hour. The fire damaged eight units, two heavily. Eight families were displaced. Fire fighters working the incident had to pull back and take shelter due to a continuation of excessive lightning once arriving on scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado developed over the rural Indian River County associated with a rapidly developing cell on the south end of a line of storms, resulting from a collision of sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen at State Road 60 and Country Road 512 observed a brief tornado touchdown in a rural area, approximately 3 miles to the east-northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced frequent cloud to ground lightning strikes across Volusia County. Two individuals were injured by lightning strikes in separate incidents about 30 minutes apart within Daytona Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man sitting next to a tree in the 1800 block of Biscayne Avenue in South Daytona Beach was struck indirectly by lightning. The man refused treatment at the scene, but was later transported to a hospital by a friend and was admitted in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced frequent cloud to ground lightning strikes across Volusia County. Two individuals were injured by lightning strikes in separate incidents about 30 minutes apart within Daytona Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was struck by lightning within the 1600 block of Dunn Avenue in Daytona Beach. She was in her apartment talking on the telephone at the time of the strike. The woman was transported to a hospital and was admitted in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper-level low over the Gulf of Mexico combined with a weak surface low near south Florida produced favorable shear conditions for shallow rotation within several onshore-moving showers and thunderstorms. One of these cells produced a brief EF0 tornado touchdown, removing large portion of roofs from several homes in Casselberry and carrying debris downstream. An isolated severe thunderstorm also produced minor wind damage in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled by high winds and landed on a home on Claracona Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southward into the central Florida Peninsula on Monday, May 18th with unseasonably strong high pressure building in behind it. An unseasonably strong non-tropical low pressure system developed over the Bahamas and began to track in a northwesterly direction toward the region. The strong pressure gradient between the high and low pressure systems brought strong winds to the coastal areas and excessive rainfall to portions of north Florida through Sunday, May 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer reported 3.69 inches of rain in a twenty four hour period ending at 6:00 a.m. EDT or 5:00 a.m. EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding across eastern Volusia County occurred as a result of nearly a week of heavy rains. The rains began on May 17 as a late season frontal boundary stalled over the region. A deep-layered low pressure system developed nearby on the 19th and plagued the region with strong and moist onshore flow and periods of training rains, until finally retrograding west into the central Gulf of Mexico on the 23rd. Rain totals in some coastal locations reached 15 to 20 inches by May 21, causing standing water levels to encroach many homes and businesses, especially in portions of Daytona Beach, Holly Hill, New Smyrna Beach, Ormond Beach and Port Orange. Additional rainfall reached 5 to 10 inches in some of these areas by the 23rd, causing water damage over 1500 homes. Less serious flooding occurred across western Volusia County and adjacent portions of Lake and Seminole Counties where storm total rains reached 12 to 16 inches. One fatality was indirectly related to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High levels of pre-existing flood waters were exacerbated as two to four inches of additional rainfall occurred in less than three hours as heavy cells trained onshore. Standing water rose to three feet or higher in several locations. Flood waters entered additional homes. Flash flood damage estimates were included collectively in the five-day flood event (May 19-24), totaling 68.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper-level low over the Gulf of Mexico combined with a weak surface low near south Florida produced favorable shear conditions for shallow rotation within several onshore-moving showers and thunderstorms. One of these cells produced a brief EF0 tornado touchdown, removing large portion of roofs from several homes in Casselberry and carrying debris downstream. An isolated severe thunderstorm also produced minor wind damage in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A EF0 tornado touched down briefly and removed the roofs from a single family home and mobile home. Portions of the roofs and other debris were carried downstream, with large metal pieces deposited in trees. Another 8 homes sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding across eastern Volusia County occurred as a result of nearly a week of heavy rains. The rains began on May 17 as a late season frontal boundary stalled over the region. A deep-layered low pressure system developed nearby on the 19th and plagued the region with strong and moist onshore flow and periods of training rains, until finally retrograding west into the central Gulf of Mexico on the 23rd. Rain totals in some coastal locations reached 15 to 20 inches by May 21, causing standing water levels to encroach many homes and businesses, especially in portions of Daytona Beach, Holly Hill, New Smyrna Beach, Ormond Beach and Port Orange. Additional rainfall reached 5 to 10 inches in some of these areas by the 23rd, causing water damage over 1500 homes. Less serious flooding occurred across western Volusia County and adjacent portions of Lake and Seminole Counties where storm total rains reached 12 to 16 inches. One fatality was indirectly related to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall over five days lead to several feet of standing water in many areas of eastern Volusia County. Rainfall totals reached 18 to 28 inches in several coastal areas. In addition to the accumulation of rain water, onshore flow and high tides over northeast Florida restricted water from draining into the intracoastal waterways, leading to a persistence of high water levels in many neighborhoods. Damage surveys determined 1531 homes were damaged in the county nearly 69 million dollars in damages and expenses. One indirect fatality was attributed to the flooding, when an intoxicated man fell into high waters and drowned. The man was last seen in Daytona Beach on May 21, and his body was discovered in Holly Hill on May 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced two brief funnel clouds near Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter located at the Wet and Wild Water Park observed a brief funnel cloud to the east. A strong thunderstorm was observed by radar in the same location at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced two brief funnel clouds near Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report and photograph was received of a funnel cloud, observed from Orlando, looking toward the west. Strong storms were occurring in this area, resulting from a recent sea breeze collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding across eastern Volusia County occurred as a result of nearly a week of heavy rains. Less serious flooding occurred across western Volusia County and adjacent portions of Lake and Seminole Counties where storm total rains reached 12 to 16 inches. One fatality was indirectly related to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy rains trained northward along the coast and contributed an additional two to four inches of rain in less than two hours. These rains caused the pre-existing high flood water levels to rise to around three feet. Flash flood damage estimates were included collectively in the five-day flood event (May 19-23), totaling 68.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of the east coast and west coast sea breeze boundaries resulted in slow moving thunderstorms over Osceola County. The storms dumped excessive rainfall in a short period of time, resulting in flooding of many homes. Some non-meteorological issues likely contributed to the property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of very heavy rain produced 4-5 inches in less than 2 hours which resulted in a rapid accumulation of deep water. One hundred and nine homes were flooded, with 16 receiving major damage. The most significant flooding affected about a 20 square block area. The flooding of the homes was likely due in part to the lack of a modern storm water management system and some storm drain blockages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm developed along the sea breeze boundary and produced large hail for several minutes in St. Lucie County. In rural Martin County, a funnel cloud was briefly observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen observed a funnel cloud form and dissipate over rural Martin County, to the west of Indiantown and east of Lake Okeechobee. The funnel lasted several minutes and was photographed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved into the Lower Mississippi River Valley from the northern Gulf of Mexico and put deep southwest flow across the area. Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area producing hail and causing a few lightning injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine power poles were downed and six more were damaged along a half-mile stretch on Dale Mabry Highway near Geraci Road. There was no damage to trees or buildings along the area. The area had been wet from rain over the previous ten days and this may have led to the poles being pushed over in the estimated 50mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter/spring drought over South Florida intensified during early parts of May, but came to a quick end as copious rainfall fell during the last 2 weeks of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought which began during the late winter months continued into early and mid May over South Florida. A very dry start to the month prompted the issuance of extreme drought (D3) conditions over virtually all of South Florida by the middle of the month. ||The onset of the rainy season around May 11 brought copious rainfall to the region as a low pressure trough in the upper levels of the atmosphere set up near South Florida, effectively ending the drought by the last week of May. Many areas received in excess of 10 inches of rain for the month, virtually all of it falling in a period of only 2 weeks. West Palm Beach recorded its second wettest May on record with 15.69 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm developed along the sea breeze boundary and produced large hail for several minutes in St. Lucie County. In rural Martin County, a funnel cloud was briefly observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A security officer at the PGA golf course in Port St. Lucie reported hail, up to the size of a quarter, falling for about five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant frontal boundary in place across north-central Florida combined with a warm, moist, unstable airmass and the east and west coast sea breeze boundaries to develop numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down briefly, snapping three power lines in an industrial park on Industrial Drive and moving through an open field before lifting and shifting its track to the southeast. The tornado then touched down again on Vanderbaker Road, moved across Grand Circle and East River Drive, before lifting along Broxburn Avenue. Several large but damaged trees were uprooted, including one that fell onto a house, numerous large branches were snapped off, and some decorative shutters were blown off of the front of a house along the path. Winds were estimated at 80mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in South Florida produce high wind gusts and hail across the Lake Okeechobee Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a queen palm and broke off several Australian Pine branches along South County Road just south of the Colony Hotel in Palm Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Five tourists were rescued from rip currents at Lauderdale-By-The-Sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds created strong rip currents at the coast of Lauderdale-By-The-Sea and 5 tourists had to be rescued during a 20 minute span in the afternoon, including one couple from Holland. The volunteer fire department conducted the rescues along with some Good Samaritans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms moved northwest across Palm Beach County for the second day in a row as the region remained under the influence of low pressure over the Southeast Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm blew down 2 trees into a parking lot at the corner of Palmetto Park Rd and Powerline Rd. One car had the back window shattered. A large branch was snapped off a pine tree at Military Trail and SW 18 ST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind gust from a thunderstorm causes damage in Belle Glade.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm in western Palm Beach County blew down a high tension power line at NW 12th and NW Main Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in South Florida produce high wind gusts and hail across the Lake Okeechobee Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A passing thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 58 mph at a mesonet site in Belle Glade. Power lines were also blown down with this storm along with a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate onshore winds caused rip currents along the coast of Broward and Miami-Dade Counties. A total of 16 people were rescued by lifeguards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people needed to be rescued on the beach in Fort Lauderdale from rip currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the Florida peninsula provided an unstable atmosphere and contributed to severe afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Sunrise Police Officer reported golf ball size hail near University Dr and Oakland Park Blvd. This is the same thunderstorm that produced a high wind gust in Tamarac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant frontal boundary in place across north-central Florida combined with a warm, moist, unstable airmass and the east and west coast sea breeze boundaries to develop numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel sized hail near mile marker 120 on Interstate 75.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail at the intersection of Palm River Road and Maydell Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant frontal boundary in place across north-central Florida combined with a warm, moist, unstable airmass and the east and west coast sea breeze boundaries to develop numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in a wooded area off of Amberly Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado lasting less than 30 seconds touched down along Smithtown Drive. Damage consisted of many large tree beanches downed, an uprooted tree, and a torn apart wooden carport. Flying debris also caused several broken windows, heavy damage to  fences, and areas of roof and carport damage. Winds were estimated at 80mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in South Florida produce high wind gusts and hail across the Lake Okeechobee Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter sized hail was reported by fire/rescue in Lakeport with a passing thunderstorm. Hail was initially penny sized, then became quarter sized as the storm intensified over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant frontal boundary in place across north-central Florida combined with a warm, moist, unstable airmass and the east and west coast sea breeze boundaries to develop numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A restaurant was completely destroyed by a lightning induced fire. The market value of the building was $132,000 while the contents were estimated at $65,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail in northwest Florida.
Afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th produced several reports of wind damage in Gadsden and Leon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down across the northern portion of Leon County between North Meridian Road and U.S. Highway 319/Thomasville Road, including Killearn Lakes.  The Leon County EOC reported about 8,000 customers without power, including Bradfordville and northeast Tallahassee.  A large tree fell on a vehicle traveling north on U.S. Highway 319 at Horseshoe Plantation Road.  The driver was seriously injured and the passenger was killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th produced several reports of wind damage in Gadsden and Leon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a house and several trees were down along Rich Bay Road.  A downed tree fell through a mobile home and injured the lone occupant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter/spring drought over South Florida intensified during early parts of May, but came to a quick end as copious rainfall fell during the last 2 weeks of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wildfire in the East Hinson Marsh area of Big Cypress National Preserve which began on April 22 was fully contained as of May 9th. A total of 30,005 acres was burned. No damage to property was noted as the fire burned over wooded areas, however the proximity of the fire to I-75 (Alligator Alley) and SR 29 caused numerous periods of road closures, especially during the late night and early morning hours due to very thick smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three children died after the boat they were in overturned during a sudden, strong thunderstorm which produced 3 to 4 foot waves on Lake George. Another nearby boat was overturned, however no one was injured. A third boat tipped severely, throwing all occupants into the water. One person sustained an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds estimated at 45 to 50 mph, produced 3-4 foot waves which capsized two boats and caused a third boat to tip severely. A 20 foot Sea Ray capsized with 2 adults and 5 children on board. All children were wearing life jackets, however two became trapped in the cabin and drowned when the boat overturned. A third child died the following day at a local hospital, from injuries sustained in the accident. One adult received minor injuries. An air-boat with 8 persons on-board also capsized, but with no injuries. A motor boat tipped severely and all on-board fell into the water. One person sustained a severed finger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter/spring drought over South Florida intensified during early parts of May, but came to a quick end as copious rainfall fell during the last 2 weeks of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought which began during the late winter months continued into early and mid May over South Florida. A very dry start to the month prompted the issuance of extreme drought (D3) conditions over virtually all of South Florida by the middle of the month. ||The onset of the rainy season around May 11 brought copious rainfall to the region as a low pressure trough in the upper levels of the atmosphere set up near South Florida, effectively ending the drought by the last week of May. Many areas received in excess of 10 inches of rain for the month, virtually all of it falling in a period of only 2 weeks. West Palm Beach recorded its second wettest May on record with 15.69 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 70 mph damaged 7 homes on Bell Creek Road. Several trees were also downed by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave and surface squall line moved across the Weather Forecast Office Jacksonville area of responsibility during the afternoon and evening of April 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny and dime size hail was falling in town of Interlachen at 4:52 p.m. EST. At 4:55 p.m. EST the Putnam County Sherrifs Office reports quarter size hail in Interlachen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary gradually settled across the area on the 23rd and 24th. The energy associated with the boundary combined with warm afternoon temperatures to produce scattered thunderstorms mainly along and ahead of the boundary. Several thunderstorms produced large hail, strong damaging winds and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the Castle Lake Mobile Home Park and the Stoneridge Landing Mobile Home Park, causing damage ranging from minor to severe to an estimated 50 mobile homes. The severe damage occurred to roofs and carports. In addition, 50 trees around the parks had the tops sheared off or twisted requiring removal of the trees. Winds were estimated at 85mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief and weak tornado touched down across Golf Crest Circle as the result of two merging thunderstorms. Three nearly 100-foot tall pine trees were blown over, one falling on a house causing major roof damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported power lines blown down near the Intersection of Washington Avenue and Elm Street. Time of based on radar imagery.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One lightning strike caused damage to five homes. One home had a hole in the roof from the strike, another had significant damage and the other three had minor damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen head of cattle, including 12 cows, two calves, and one bull, all humpbacked Brahmans, were killed when lightning struck the field in which the herd was standing. Time is estimated based off of radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As major Hurricane Bill moved north over the Atlantic, paralleling the Florida coast approximately 900 miles offshore, rough surf occurred along the east central Florida beaches. Long period swells began increasing at the beaches late Friday, August 21, with breaking surf peaking at 5 to 6 feet during the morning and afternoon of Saturday August 22. One person drowned while body-boarding in the rough surf in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Orlando man who was body-boarding in swells on Bethune Beach in New Smyrna Beach associated with distant Hurricane Bill, was battered by large waves as he ventured out further than his family into the pounding surf. He was then washed back towards shore unconscious and later pronounced dead at a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of mid to upper level troughs over the eastern Gulf of Mexico and south Florida contributed to the formation of severe thunderstorms over southeast portions of Florida during the afternoon hours. The storms caused large hail, strong wind gusts and severe flooding. More than 15,000 customers lost electrical service in Miami-Dade County during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flooding affected the Mid and South Beach sections of Miami Beach as well as downtown Miami from a nearly stationary thunderstorm. A total of 9.30 inches fell at the cooperative station on South Beach, most of this falling in less than 3 hours. This caused as much as 3 feet of standing water on streets and garages on South Beach, resulting in many vehicles stalled on streets and road closures across the area. Cars were seen floating down Michigan Avenue at 11th Street. Several businesses had water intrusion along Alton Road and 17th Street. A number of condominium buildings along West Avenue had up to 5 feet of water in the parking garages, resulting in dozens of cars being towed. Significant flooded was also reported in downtown Miami in the Omni area.||At the Fountainbleau Hotel on Miami Beach, heavy rains caused an eight-foot hole to open up in the lobby's ceiling, causing ankle deep water to spread from the lobby into a nightclub.

Several trees were snapped or uprooted, and there was damage to fences, sheds, well-anchored playground equipment, and a screen porch was destroyed also causing minor roof damage where it was attached to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was in place across central Florida with an upper trough over the region moving upper disturbances over the area allowing for numerous showers and thunderstorms. A widespread 4 to 8 inches of rain fell across the Tampa Bay area resulting in some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rains totalling 4 to 8 inches across the county resulted in some road closures on State Roads 70, 62, 39 and County Road 675. The Manatee Dam was forced to release approximately 11,000cfs from the pool to slow the rise in the elevation at the dam and this caused the third highest crest in history at the Manatee River at Rye Bridge, peaking at 17.85 feet at 1815EST. At this height, water reaches the foundations of elevated homes and the Christian Retreat Center. There was widespread flooding in the Samoset neighborhood that resulted in several homes receiving water damage to flooring and furniture.

One 40-year-old woman felt lightning move through her arm as she was holding an umbrella next to a truck. Another 34-year-old woman felt lightning come through her keyboard while she was on her computer. Both were transported to the hospital in stable condition.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds damaged two businesses in Tallahassee, the Alltel/Warrior Boxing Systems building on South Monroe Street and the Toys R Us store on Apalachee Parkway.  Portions of the roofs of both buildings were ripped off, with the most significant structural damage at Toys R Us.  Several vehicles in the toy store parking lot were damaged.  Debris was scattered throughout the Kmart shopping plaza onto Apalachee Parkway.  Several trees fell across Apalachee Parkway.  Three vehicles crashed into each other trying to avoid the downed trees.  None of the motorists were injured.  Apalachee Parkway and Old St Augustine Road were closed for several hours as city crews removed the fallen trees.  More than 2,000 customers in Indian Head Acres were without power.  Numerous trees were snapped or toppled throughout the Indian Head Acres subdivision, and extended around a half mile south of the Toys R Us and east to a half mile west of Blairstone Road.  The most significant tree damage occurred along Old St Augustine Road about one block east of Lafayette Street and on Apakin Nene Road.  Damage in this area was consistent with sustained winds of 75 mph with gusts to 85 mph.

The person reporting the damage stated that several people saw a 8-10 foot funnel on the ground. ||A roof was lifted off of a trailer at 1256 Milum Drive, with minor porch and gutter damage to other trailers at the park. Eyewitnesses also reported hail and minor flooding.
