EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved south down the Florida peninsula during the early morning hours of June 23 in association with a strong mid level low pressure trough. This cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms swept across southeast Florida during the morning hours causing widespread high winds and damage. Over 30,000 customers lost power in southeast Florida during this morning event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof of a house and ignited a fire, injuring a resident of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was dissipating across the area with high pressure ridging across southern Florida. This kept west to southwest winds in place across the local area and allowed for numerous thunderstorms with heavy rains and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Tampa International Airport recorded 4.72 inches of rain, most of which fell in several hours.  Roadwork near the entrance to the airport was under major construction at the time leading to poor drainage.  The road was closed for 90 minutes due to stalled vehicles in the water on the road.  The AAA Auto Club South reported 200 service calls during the event, many for stalled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another house and car was struck by lightning in South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home on northwest 62 court was struck by lighting. The strike shattered 2 windows and produced burn marks on parts of the home. Most electronics in the dwelling were also inoperable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another a-typical cold front stalled across north Florida. Westerly flow aloft increased and brought scattered showers and thunderstorms onshore throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines and trees were down near 78th St and Causeway Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed for an early sea breeze development near the coast. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the boundary and allowed several waterspouts to form along with some strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout formed north of the Courtney Campbell Causeway and moved southeast across the road then dissipated just south of the causeway.  Photographs from the public suggest the spout crossed the causeway.  There were no reports of damage to vehicles as traffic slowed or stopped on either side of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms cause road closures and damage by heavy rainfall and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches during the afternoon of the 2nd caused Fisheating Creek to spill over its banks and flood nearby roadways. Additional roadways were also closed across Glades County after the afternoon and evening rainfall. County officials closed portions of State Roads 29 and 74 and a couple of secondary roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms cause road closures and damage by heavy rainfall and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Beach Fire Rescue responded to a call of a lightning strike that hit an unoccupied residence and blew a 5 foot hole into the chimney. There was no fire or injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail, high winds, and lightning affect metropolitan sections of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 61 MPH at the Boca Raton Community High School. At the Florida Atlantic campus the public reported two transformers blown out along with a 20 to 30 feet tall tree, 6 inches in diameter blown over. Also, at the Boca Raton town center the public estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH that was accompanied with pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another house and car was struck by lightning in South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man in a Chevy Impala was traveling northbound on Interstate 75 when the vehicle was struck by lightning. The strike left the car inoperable and smoking, but the driver was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail, high winds, and lightning affect metropolitan sections of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Palm Beach County Sheriff���s Office deputy responded to a minor car accident and was nearly struck by lightning while on the scene of the wreck. The strike knocked the deputy to the ground and had to be rushed to West Boca Community Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail and high winds affect Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches knocked out of trees at Nob Hill Road and Cleary Blvd. The spotter also recorded 59 MPH winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area in the Gulf of Mexico produced an unstable and moist southwest wind flow across south Florida, leading to active afternoon and evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half a mile north of the Weston town center the public reported a wind gust to 60mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area in the Gulf of Mexico produced an unstable and moist southwest wind flow across south Florida, leading to active afternoon and evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired air force personnel reported wind gusts in excess of 50 knots along with power out at the intersection of University and Broward Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area in the Gulf of Mexico produced an unstable and moist southwest wind flow across south Florida, leading to active afternoon and evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 mph was reported by a mesonet at the Broward Admin Building just west of downtown Fort Lauderdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast produce damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home in Lake Worth was struck by lightning, and the strike blew out pieces of concrete from a corner of the house. No injuries were reported. Also multiple grass fires were started across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast produce damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a transformer at a sugar mill in the Belle Glade area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast produce damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a car traveling eastbound on Interstate 595 just west on Highway 1. There were 3 people in the car, but no one was injured. The strike busted out the rear window of the car, shorted out the electrical system, and blew out a tire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area in the Gulf of Mexico produced an unstable and moist southwest wind flow across south Florida, leading to active afternoon and evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported pea sized hail and trees down along State Road 84 and 136th Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts and hail occur from the Lake Okeechobee region to the southeast coast metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust from a thunderstorm in Clewiston ripped a house awning off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail falls in southern sections of Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel-sized hail near SW 70 Ave and SW 22 Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast produce damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported nickel sized hail along the Florida Turnpike and Lake Worth Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of the wet season in South Florida produce more lightning damage along with hail and high wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a plumbing stack of a house near the intersection of NW 165 Street and NW 30th Avenue, and a neighbor heard the loud thunder and saw smoke coming from the house. The neighbor then called 911. A fire started in the kitchen and the firefighters put out the fire. No one was home at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of the wet season in South Florida produce more lightning damage along with hail and high wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a gas station in Miramar blowing a hole in the roof and knocking out power. The gas station attendant saw the flash and heard the thunder, but was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colliding sea breeze boundaries produced a narrow line of intense storms from southwest Volusia County to southwest Orange County. Reports of penny to nickel sized hail were received from Volusia and Orange Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter observed dime to nickel sized hail falling near the intersection of State Road 415 and Reed Ellis Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved south down the Florida peninsula during the early morning hours of June 23 in association with a strong mid level low pressure trough. This cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms swept across southeast Florida during the morning hours causing widespread high winds and damage. Over 30,000 customers lost power in southeast Florida during this morning event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was dissipating across the area with high pressure ridging across southern Florida. This kept west to southwest winds in place across the local area and allowed for numerous thunderstorms with heavy rains and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of rain caused flooding of roads between Hudson and Port Richey.  One neighborhood near the intersection of Ranch Road and Zimmerman Road was isolated due to water on roads.  About 10 homes were evacauted and two homes had water damage on the first floor.  Emergency management states this is a know low lying area in the county and this happens every 3 to 5 years.  The damage amount was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was dissipating across the area with high pressure ridging across southern Florida. This kept west to southwest winds in place across the local area and allowed for numerous thunderstorms with heavy rains and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river stage on the Little Manatee River near Wimauma reached 14.15 feet during the evening of July 2nd.  Flood stage is 11 feet.   The flooding was a result of two days of heavy rain.  The total rainfall at the National Weather Service in Ruskin from June 30 through July 1 was 4.53 inches. Fire Rescue reported Owens Road was washed away in the area of the river and numerous roads were inaccessible due to flooding.  The damage amount listed is a rough estimate of the road damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed for scattered afternoon thunderstorms to develop along sea breeze and outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck in a soccer field as a church group was in the midst of a game and nearby picnic. This strike was a bolt from the blue as there was no rain in the immediate area.  Radar detected the core of the storm to be six miles to the south of the location.  Eighteen victims were transported to area hospitals where one died from his injuries.  Another nine victims were treated on-site with symptoms consistent with a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved across St George Island State Park.  It capsized a boat and downed some tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout came ashore as an EF0 tornado and moved across St George Island State Park.  It capsized a boat and downed some tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous bands of heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms trained over interior portions of St. Lucie County from late afternoon until well into the evening, producing over 12 inches of rain in less than 24 hours. Temporary roadway and lowland flooding resulted, but no structures were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large area of 5 to 13 inches of rain fell from near the Florida Turnpike, impacting western portions of Port St. Lucie, and inland to the rural portions of central and western St. Lucie County. A rainfall spotter located 4 miles west of Port St. Lucie recorded over 12 inches of rain in less than 24 hours and 6 inches in 90 minutes during the evening. Also, a South Florida Water Management Rain Gage (C24SE; 4 miles north-northwest of the Traditions Community within Port St. Lucie) recorded a 24-hour total of 12.99 inches (including 10.74 inches in four hours, 1745 to 2145 EST and 5.47 inches in one hour, 2030-2130 EST). While temporary roadway, lowland and urban flooding was extensive, no homes or businesses were damaged. Standing water levels reached up to three feet on some roadways and yards in and near the Traditions Community, causing many homes and schools to become briefly cut-off from surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level high pressure system was over the U.S. and an upper level trough was pushing offshore of the Atlantic seaboard. A quasi-stationary surface front was across north Florida and became diffuse. Scattered afternoon storms developed over an unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the construction crane on the Flagler Beach Pier. Four workers received a slight electric jolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia and north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported a roof blown off a house near Live Oak. Damage pictures revealed a weakly constructed section of metal sheet roof was lifted off of a portion of the home by thunderstorm wind gusts. Doppler radar indicated velocities around 40 to 45 knots around 2 to 3 kft agl across the area.  The damage was isolated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm tides associated with Tropical Storm Ida ranged from three to five feet along the Florida Panhandle coast during the morning of November 10.  Coastal flooding closed several roads in Wakulla County.  In Franklin County, flooding and debris closed Alligator Point Road and Water Road in Apalachicola.  Storm tide flooding closed Indian Pass Road and the Stumphole area on Cape San Blas in Gulf County.  Minor coastal flooding was reported at Santa Rosa Beach in Walton County, and Panama City Beach in Bay County.  Minor beach erosion was reported in Bay, Franklin and Wakulla counties.
Repeated torrential rains over the past few days caused extensive areal and riverine flooding in Washington, Leon and Calhoun counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated torrential rainfall the past few days caused moderate to major flooding along the Choctawhatchee River, which pushed water over County Road 279 south of Caryville.  Areal flooding was also observed along Holmes Creek at Vernon, and at Ebro and New Hope.  More than a dozen homes were impacted.  In Caryville, the post office and city hall were closed.  The volunteer fire department and work camp were relocated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated torrential rains over the past few days caused extensive areal and riverine flooding in Washington, Leon and Calhoun counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areal flooding from torrential rainfall of the past few days closed many county and secondary roads, including Fairbanks Ferry Road, County Road 12 from North Meridian Road to U.S. Highway 319, Crooked Road south of the Lake Talquin dam at State Road 20, North Meridian Road at Double Bridges, Chadwick Road, and Old Bainbridge Road north of U.S. Highway 27.  Moderate to major flooding along the Ochlockonee River affected many homes, with some evacuations reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated torrential rains over the past few days caused extensive areal and riverine flooding in Washington, Leon and Calhoun counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding along the Chipola River impacted at least 150 homes from Altha south to the Gulf County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated heavy rainfall caused additional areal and riverine flooding in portions of Washington, Madison and Wakulla counties during the first week of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly man drowned outside his home near the Blue Springs boat landing on the Withlacoochee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms early on the 16th produced large hail near Tallahassee and several reports of wind damage over portions of Leon and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down.  Ten homes were damaged from fallen trees and straight line winds: two on Old Woodville Highway, three on Woodville Highway, and one each on Wakulla Springs Road, Grubbs Road, Register Road, Barwick Drive and Natural Bridge Road.  Over 4,000 residents in Woodville and surrounding areas were without power.  A large tree limb was blown through a mobile home wall on Spring Sink Road.  Road closures due to fallen trees and power lines included Woodville Highway from Robinson Road to Lawhon and Oak Ridge Road, and Natural Bridge Road from Old Woodville Highway to a mile and a half east of Woodville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line with scattered severe storms moved across the area early in the morning. Jet dynamics were strong as well as low level helicities, however surface based instability was lacking. A few low topped supercells moved across the Suwanee River Valley, with some wind damage reported near Live Oak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs about 4 to 6 inches in diameter were broken and strewn about Nobles Ferry Road, just northwest of downtown Live Oak. The time of the event was based on Doppler Radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ida, a Category Two hurricane, weakened as it approached the northern Gulf Coast on November 9.  It came ashore over the western Florida Panhandle coast about 30 miles east-southeast of Mobile, Alabama during the morning of November 10.  Ida then became extratropical and moved northeast across central Alabama and northern Georgia during the next 24 to 36 hours.  Both the maximum sustained wind (38 knots) and peak wind gust (49 knots) were recorded at Buoy 42039, located 80 miles south of Panama City, Florida.  Rainfall totals from 0700 LST November 9th through 0700 LST November 11th varied from one to two inches over the Florida Big Bend, to three to five inches over the Florida Panhandle.  Storm tides along the coast ranged from three to five feet, along with minor beach erosion.  Coastal and inland flooding closed several roads, with county schools and non-essential government offices also closed on November 10.  Power outages from downed trees and power lines affected portions of Bay, Gulf, Leon, Franklin and Wakulla counties.  The Walton County EOC issued a voluntary evacuation for residents in low-lying areas and mobile homes.  One family was evacuated from Alligator Point.  A Gulf County home was damaged by a fallen tree.  A local state of emergency was declared in Wakulla County.

Strong rip currents along the Florida Panhandle beaches cause drowning deaths along Okaloosa County beaches in the Destin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Joseph Jones, a 39 year old from Riverdale, Georgia, was pronounced dead at the hospital after being caught in a strong rip current at Destin Beach around 5 PM CDT.  He entered the surf to help two young girls that were caught in the rip current.

Repeated heavy rainfall caused additional areal and riverine flooding in portions of Washington, Madison and Wakulla counties during the first week of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to record flooding along the Withlacoochee River closed several roads, including County Roads 145 and 150 east of Pinetta to the bridge.  Many homes along the river were flooded, with evacuations on April 5 and 6.  Oakhill Road flooded at 65 feet.  Several dirt roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents claimed the lives of two men at Panama City Beach on the afternoon of October 5th.  There was a high risk for rip currents and double red flags were posted by beach officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man drowned while swimming in rough surf at Panama City Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents claimed the lives of two men at Panama City Beach on the afternoon of October 5th.  There was a high risk for rip currents and double red flags were posted by beach officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man drowned while swimming in rough surf at Panama City Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm on the 10th produced intermittent wind damage from east of Tallahassee to near Greenville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down between Aucilla Highway and Interstate 10 in the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm on the 10th produced intermittent wind damage from east of Tallahassee to near Greenville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down just west of State Highway 140 to the northwest of Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms early on the 16th produced large hail near Tallahassee and several reports of wind damage over portions of Leon and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported at the C.H. Corn Hydroelectric Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms early on the 16th produced large hail near Tallahassee and several reports of wind damage over portions of Leon and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms early on the 16th produced large hail near Tallahassee and several reports of wind damage over portions of Leon and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down south of Woodville, with seven roads closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents along the Florida Panhandle beaches cause drowning deaths along Okaloosa County beaches in the Destin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Airman First Class Josh Roussell of the 46th Test Wing on Eglin Air Force Base died shortly after 6 PM CDT after he was pulled out in a rip current while playing football in waist-deep water with his brother-in-law.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents along the Western Florida Panhandle become deadly at Destin on July 3rd when a tourist visiting from Louisiana drowns at James Lee Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Albert Mullally Jr of Morgan City Louisiana drowned just after 6 PM CDT while swimming in the surf at James Lee Park in Destin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Claudette formed as tropical depression about 90 miles west-southwest of Tampa during the early morning hours of August 16th.  Claudette moved to the northwest and strengthened to a tropical storm later in the morning.  A peak wind gust of 51 knots was recorded at Alligator Point in Franklin County.  Claudette came ashore along the Florida Panhandle coast near Fort Walton Beach early on August 17th.  The lowest sea-level pressure was 1007.7 mb at Destin/Fort Walton Beach Airport.  Storm total rainfall ranged from one to four inches.  Storm tides ranged were two to four feet along the Panhandle coastline.  Minor beach erosion was observed along coastal Gulf County, with minor to moderate beach erosion along coastal Walton County.  A man drowned as a result of the strong surf at Panama City Beach.  Minor coastal flooding occurred in Wakulla County.  Sporadic wind damage was reported in Bay, Gulf and Franklin counties.  More than 9,000 residents were without power from the storm.  St Andrews, St Joseph Peninsula and St George Island state park officials evacuated visitors and closed campgrounds.

Storm tides associated with Tropical Storm Claudette as it moved into the Florida Panhandle ranged from two to four feet along the coastline from the evening of the 16th into the pre-dawn hours of the 17th.  Minor to moderate beach erosion affected the coastline of Walton County.  Estimates of 30 to 40 feet of beach sand was lost at the West Watersound Beach and areas along Alys Beach.  Along coastal Gulf County, minor beach erosion was reported.

Lightning struck the attic of a home in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree near the home and ran up the aluminum porch post to a gas line in the attic.  There were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure at the surface and aloft centered over Florida provided record warm temperatures from October 7th to October 14th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record high temperatures were felt for four days in a row from October 8th through October 11th at the Lakeland Linder Airport. The high temperature was 95 degrees each afternoon from the 8th through the 10th and 94 degrees on the 11th. This record heat combined with afternoon dew points in the 70s to allow for heat indicies of 100-110 for a few hours each afternoon. As a result, one person in Davenport died on October 9th from heat stroke after working outside all day and then playing basketball that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering easterly swells of 2-3 feet created an elevated rip current risk along the north Florida Atlantic coast. Surface winds were northeast around 10-15 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a couple was caught in a rip current at Crescent Beach. One person returned to shore; the other drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Claudette was the third named tropical cyclone in the Atlantic Basin for 2009, forming over the eastern Gulf of Mexico at Tropical Depression Four early on the morning of August 16th, becoming a Tropical Storm south of Apalachicola, Florida later that day, then moving onshore as a tropical storm on Santa Rosa Island between Destin and Okaloosa Island Pier shortly after midnight CDT on August 17.  Claudette weakened to a tropical depression over South Central Alabama around daybreak on August 17th.  Bands of heavy rainfall on the north side of Claudette produced areas of flooding over portions of Okaloosa County, with minor to moderate beach erosion due to high surf being reported around the Destin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor beach erosion and coastal flooding was reported along the Gulf beaches around Destin associated with the landfall of Tropical Storm Claudette.  Maximum tides 2.3 feet above normal were reported by a tidal gauge at the East Pass in Destin.

Claudette weakened to a tropical depression over South Central Alabama around daybreak on August 17th.  Bands of heavy rainfall on the north side of Claudette produced areas of flooding over portions of Okaloosa County, with minor to moderate beach erosion due to high surf being reported around the Destin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sherman Kennedy Road north of Baker was closed to through traffic due to excessive washing of the roadway.  The closed area was between John Riley Barnhill Road and Kilcrease Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore winds and a small, persistent swell combined to produce numerous strong rip currents along portions of the Florida east coast. Many beach-goers entered the surf due to warm summer-like temperatures. Volusia County Beach Patrol rescued several swimmers from rip currents, however one man drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55 year old man from Indiana and his brother waded into waist deep water on Ormond Beach at the Granada Boulevard Approach. Both men were overcome by a rip current. The man was rescued by a life guard and left in the care of bystanders while the life guard re-entered the surf and saved the man's brother. In the meantime, the first victim stopped breathing and was transported to the hospital where he was pronounced dead. Several others were also rescued from rip currents across the county this day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure built in to the north of the Jacksonville area with a coastal trough setting up along the Florida coast. Strong northeast winds created a low level convergent flow across north Florida coming in off the Atlantic Ocean while the upper level flow remained southwesterly setting up mid level ventilation to produce a heavy rainfall event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteers in Jacksonville Beach measured 24 hour rainfall totals of 3.82 and 3.85 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure built in to the north of the Jacksonville area with a coastal trough setting up along the Florida coast. Strong northeast winds created a low level convergent flow across north Florida coming in off the Atlantic Ocean while the upper level flow remained southwesterly setting up mid level ventilation to produce a heavy rainfall event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteers on the south side of Jacksonville measured 24-hour rainfall amounts of 2.58 to 3.18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure built in to the north of the Jacksonville area with a coastal trough setting up along the Florida coast. Strong northeast winds created a low level convergent flow across north Florida coming in off the Atlantic Ocean while the upper level flow remained southwesterly setting up mid level ventilation to produce a heavy rainfall event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 2.40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms over eastern sections of Palm Beach County produced lightning strikes which affected two houses in the Lake Worth area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit two houses about a block apart from each other at nearly the same time in Lake Worth. A house at 185 Bryn Mawr Drive had roof damage from a fallen tree which knocked a three to four feet wide hole in the living room ceiling and started a small fire in the attic. The hole in the roof allowed rainfall in and that subsequently put out the fire. The other house at 185 Vassar Drive suffered shattered roof tiles and also started a small fire in the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture south of a trough across central Florida produced scattered to numerous thunderstorms along coastal sections of southeast Florida during the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boater was standing in a 14-foot aluminum hull boat when a thunderstorm approached. The man was struck and killed. Two others were with him but in the water, and they were not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal convection developed over north Florida during the afternoon. Surface based CAPES were near 4000 J/kg with mid level shear around 40-50 kts in advance of the approaching front. Isolated severe storms developed in advance of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts blew down a small barn. Debris from the small barn was blown into a larger barn.  There was substantial damage to the structures, but the cost of damage was unknown. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ida, a Category Two hurricane, weakened as it approached the northern Gulf Coast on November 9.  It came ashore over the western Florida Panhandle coast about 30 miles east-southeast of Mobile, Alabama during the morning of November 10.  Ida then became extratropical and moved northeast across central Alabama and northern Georgia during the next 24 to 36 hours.  Both the maximum sustained wind (38 knots) and peak wind gust (49 knots) were recorded at Buoy 42039, located 80 miles south of Panama City, Florida.  Rainfall totals from 0700 LST November 9th through 0700 LST November 11th varied from one to two inches over the Florida Big Bend, to three to five inches over the Florida Panhandle.  Storm tides along the coast ranged from three to five feet, along with minor beach erosion.  Coastal and inland flooding closed several roads, with county schools and non-essential government offices also closed on November 10.  Power outages from downed trees and power lines affected portions of Bay, Gulf, Leon, Franklin and Wakulla counties.  The Walton County EOC issued a voluntary evacuation for residents in low-lying areas and mobile homes.  One family was evacuated from Alligator Point.  A Gulf County home was damaged by a fallen tree.  A local state of emergency was declared in Wakulla County.

Storm tides associated with Tropical Storm Ida ranged from three to five feet along the Florida Panhandle coast during the morning of November 10.  Coastal flooding closed several roads in Wakulla County.  In Franklin County, flooding and debris closed Alligator Point Road and Water Road in Apalachicola.  Storm tide flooding closed Indian Pass Road and the Stumphole area on Cape San Blas in Gulf County.  Minor coastal flooding was reported at Santa Rosa Beach in Walton County, and Panama City Beach in Bay County.  Minor beach erosion was reported in Bay, Franklin and Wakulla counties.
A severe thunderstorm on the 10th produced intermittent wind damage from east of Tallahassee to near Greenville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few utility poles were down south of U.S. Highway 27 just west of Chaires Crossroads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 3.51 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteers measured 2.46 and 2.47 inches of rainfall in 24 hrs in Orange Park.

A surface trough stretched from the mid-Atlantic seaboard southwest across south Georgia while a lingering outflow boundary stretched across central Florida. An unstable airmass fueld convection as well as the above surface features during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported penny size hail along A1A as he was driving into Amelia Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm which produced gale force wind gusts across Key West Harbor produced isolated minor wind damage along the north shore of Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were down on a residential street in the Key Haven subdivision on Stock Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short wave trough energy combined over a moist and unstable airmass across north and central Florida to yield scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down at the Champions Club Golf Course in Julington Creek. One as big as 8-10 inches in diameter. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave and surface squall line moved across the Weather Forecast Office Jacksonville area of responsibility during the afternoon and evening of April 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado caused damage to homes on Gassett Road with part of a roof blown off a house and a wood fence was blown into a yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several funnel cloud reports were received associated with afternoon thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County Sheriffs Office relayed a public report of a funnel cloud on the east side of the Saint  Johns River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The sheriff���s office in Moore Haven reported hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff office in Moore Haven reported nickel hail falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line well ahead of a cold front produced numerous thunderstorms with damaging straight-line winds, large hail and a brief, weak tornado. Several Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) features were apparent on radar with rotating comma head signatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of straight-line thunderstorm winds damaged several residences within the Colony Park Mobile Home Village just north of North Courtney Parkway and North Tropical Trail. Damage was consistent with 55-65 mph straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough and in concert with an approaching cold front combined over a moist and unstable airmass to yield scattered severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on power lines which caused power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding in portions of the western Florida panhandle.
Late afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th produced hail and damaging winds in parts of Wakulla and Leon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Bradfordville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced numerous reports of damaging winds and a few weak tornadoes across the Panhandle during the morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down near Interstate 10 mile marker 74 just west of Mossy Head.  It uprooted numerous trees, which closed several lanes.  No traffic accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing area of low pressure was in place over the eastern Gulf of Mexico, sending bands of showers and thunderstorms over the area and increasing winds across the area. With the storms, an isolated tornado briefly touched down in Cape Coral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty three homes were damaged from an EF-0 tornado with winds estimated at 70 to 80mph. Eleven homes suffered the most damage mainly to roofs, fascia, soffits, broken windows, and outdoor equipment such as satellite and electric systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary gradually settled across the area on the 23rd and 24th. The energy associated with the boundary combined with warm afternoon temperatures to produce scattered thunderstorms mainly along and ahead of the boundary. Several thunderstorms produced large hail, strong damaging winds and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported on County Highway 581.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second mid/upper level trough moved down the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours of June 23. This triggered a second round of severe and damaging thunderstorms over much of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch broke off and pierced a three-foot hole in the ceiling of an apartment in the Sunview Park area of west Fort Lauderdale. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms on the 2nd produced damaging winds, large hail, flooding and a tornado across much of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water closed State Road 79 and Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail and high winds affect Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail the size of pennies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS crossed over southeast Georgia during the morning. The remnant upper level short wave trough crossed over northeast Florida during the afternoon which destabilized the atmosphere significantly to yield numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening.  The modified KJAX 12Z sounding indicated surface based lifted indices of -8 and CAPES of 4200 J/kg were possible during maximum heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down with a lot of debris in a neighborhood off of County Road 252 near the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid to upper level disturbances moved across Florida from the Gulf of Mexico ahead of a frontal system over the central United States, causing periods of very heavy rainfall and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a condominium complex at 3531 SW 10 Street collapsed during heavy early morning rain, causing water to enter all 24 units. The roof had been under repair but was not covered when the heavy rain occurred.||Rainfall amounts in the area were generally in the 2 to 3 inch range throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th produced hail and damaging winds in parts of Wakulla and Leon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Bannerman Road and Summerbrooke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level impulses moved over a stationary frontal boundary across north Florida and southeast Georgia producing severe weather and flooding.  This event continued into April 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 17 year old man was struck by lightning at 1512 Dahlgreen Street. He was treated at the scene by Fire-Rescue and then transported to Baptist South Hospital. During the storm the young man ran outside to bring his dogs in from the storm, when he was struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved south down the Florida peninsula during the early morning hours of June 23 in association with a strong mid level low pressure trough. This cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms swept across southeast Florida during the morning hours causing widespread high winds and damage. Over 30,000 customers lost power in southeast Florida during this morning event.
A low pressure system remained over the northern Gulf keeping the area in deep moist southerly flow. Numerous thunderstorms moved over the area as a result and spawned a tornado and some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public had photographic evidence of a tornado in west Clearwater that dissipated just before moving offshore. No damage was reported along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCS accompanied by a weakening shortwave trough provided enough destabilization to support severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter saw a funnel cloud near Radcliff Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved into the Lower Mississippi River Valley from the northern Gulf of Mexico and put deep southwest flow across the area. Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area producing hail and causing a few lightning injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous public reports of penny to nickel size hail across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms over the Gulf of Mexico produced outflows during the morning that converged with the east coast sea breeze during the early afternoon. A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported significant flooding mainly off of Blanding Blvd. from Orange Park toward Middleburg. Culverts were overflowed on streets with overflow water moving very quickly. Significant soil erosion occurred along Johns Avenue, and a fence was washed down. Water also flooded a car along Johns Avenue. Old Jennings Road was flooded from Blanding Blvd (State Road 21) to Brannon Field Road, and along portions of Buggy Whip Trail. Tynes Road was also flooded. A spotter measured 5.74 inches of rainfall in two hours near Arora and Blanding Blvd. Doppler radar storm total amounts indicated about 4 to 5 inches over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A downburst from a severe thunderstorm on the 28th caused extensive damage to a warehouse, and downed numerous trees and power lines in Marianna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down across the center of the city.  Several homes, apartments and vehicles were damaged by fallen trees.  Power outages affected much of the city.  A large section of roof from a warehouse at the intersection of Franklin and Jefferson Street was peeled off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north to south line of thunderstorms developed rapidly over central Orange County, including metro Orlando as the west coast sea breeze boundary intersected another mesoscale boundary. A funnel cloud associated with a strong storm tracked just south of Highway 528, then touched down briefly and heavily damaged the roof of a large warehouse. Excessive lightning, nearly 2200 cloud to ground strikes in an hour, affected central Orange County. Lightning struck a apartment complex north of Orlando and started a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado associated with a rapidly developing thunderstorm touched down briefly and heavily damaged the roof of the Ikon Office Solutions warehouse. Large portions of the aluminum roof, and insulation were pealed away, with much of the debris falling into the warehouse. Two trailers adjacent to the building were also knocked onto their sides and a pickup truck was heavily damaged from flying debris. NWS officials rated the tornado as EF0, with winds estimated at 65 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS crossed over southeast Georgia during the morning. The remnant upper level short wave trough crossed over northeast Florida during the afternoon which destabilized the atmosphere significantly to yield numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening.  The modified KJAX 12Z sounding indicated surface based lifted indices of -8 and CAPES of 4200 J/kg were possible during maximum heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at Mill Street and 1st Street in White Springs.
